name: Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis)
description: Progressive restriction of glenohumeral joint motion due to capsular fibrosis and chronic inflammation.
key_findings: Global restriction of glenohumeral motion (capsular pattern: ER > ABD > IR). Pain worse at night. Three phases: painful, freezing, thawing.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from rotator cuff tear by preserved power in restricted range. Night pain is hallmark. Rule out glenohumeral OA via X-ray. Check blood glucose — high association with diabetes.

interventions: Pain education and reassurance about natural history
Gentle pendular exercises within pain-free range
Corticosteroid injection for pain relief (1-2 injections)
Grade III-IV capsular stretching (ER, IR, ABD)

special_tests.name: External Rotation Range Test
special_tests.description: Measure passive ER in 0° abduction. <50% compared to uninvolved side indicates restriction.

special_tests.name: Apley Scratch Test
special_tests.description: Patient reaches over shoulder and under arm. Asymmetry indicates limitation.

special_tests.name: Passive Abduction Test
special_tests.description: Passive abduction restriction with firm end-feel. Capsular pattern present.

name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy
description: Degeneration or irritation of rotator cuff tendons, commonly supraspinatus, due to overload and reduced vascularity.
key_findings: Painful arc 60-120°, positive impingement tests, weakness in resisted external rotation and abduction. Tenderness at greater tuberosity.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from full-thickness tear by strength testing (weakness suggests tear). Painful arc is characteristic. USS/MRI for structural confirmation. Check scapular kinematics — often dyskinetic.

interventions: Isotonic rotator cuff strengthening (ER, IR, ABD)
Serratus anterior and lower trapezius activation
Return to activity with load monitoring

special_tests.name: Hawkins-Kennedy Test
special_tests.description: Forward flex shoulder to 90°, internally rotate. Pain indicates impingement.

special_tests.name: Neer's Sign
special_tests.description: Stabilize scapula, passively flex arm. Pain indicates supraspinatus impingement.

special_tests.name: Empty Can Test (Jobe's)
special_tests.description: Resist abduction at 90° in scapular plane with IR. Weakness/pain indicates supraspinatus involvement.

name: Rotator Cuff Full-Thickness Tear
description: Complete disruption of one or more rotator cuff tendons, most commonly supraspinatus.
key_findings: Significant weakness (especially ER and ABD), positive drop arm test, possible palpable gap. Pseudoparalysis in massive tears.
diagnostic_tips: Drop arm test + weakness distinguishes from tendinopathy. Lag signs highly specific. Order MRI for surgical planning in young active patients. In elderly, asymptomatic tears are common — correlate with clinical picture.

interventions: Scapular stabilisation and remaining cuff strengthening
Functional strengthening and return to sport (6-12 months)

special_tests.name: Drop Arm Test
special_tests.description: Passively abduct to 90°, ask patient to slowly lower. Inability or painful drop indicates full-thickness tear.

special_tests.name: External Rotation Lag Sign
special_tests.description: Hold near full ER, release. Lag (inability to maintain ER) indicates posterior cuff tear.

special_tests.name: Hornblower's Sign
special_tests.description: Resist ER with arm in 90° ABD and 90° elbow flexion. Weakness indicates teres minor involvement.

name: Subacromial Impingement Syndrome
description: Mechanical compression of subacromial structures (bursa, rotator cuff) under the coracoacromial arch during shoulder elevation.
key_findings: Painful arc 60-120°, pain with overhead activities, positive Neer and Hawkins-Kennedy tests, tender subacromial space.
diagnostic_tips: Impingement is a syndrome not a diagnosis. Identify the underlying cause (weakness, GIRD, posture, scapular dyskinesis). X-ray to assess acromion morphology. Distinguish from rotator cuff tear.

special_tests.name: Hawkins-Kennedy Test
special_tests.description: Flex shoulder 90°, internally rotate. Pain in subacromial region is positive.

special_tests.name: Neer's Sign
special_tests.description: Stabilise scapula, passively forward flex. Pain indicates subacromial impingement.

special_tests.name: Painful Arc Test
special_tests.description: Active shoulder abduction. Pain between 60-120° indicates subacromial pathology.

name: Shoulder Instability (Anterior)
description: Inability to maintain the humeral head in the glenoid fossa due to laxity or labral/capsular deficiency — most commonly anterior.
key_findings: Apprehension with ER in abduction, positive relocation test, sulcus sign if multidirectional. History of dislocation.
diagnostic_tips: Apprehension is the key sign (not just pain). Differentiate traumatic from atraumatic instability. Assess for Beighton hypermobility. MRI arthrogram for labral assessment.

interventions: 3 weeks sling immobilisation (external rotation position reduces recurrence)
Bankart repair for young athletes (<25 years)
Latarjet for recurrent instability with bone loss

special_tests.name: Apprehension Test
special_tests.description: Abduct arm 90°, apply ER with anterior directed force. Apprehension (not just pain) is positive.

special_tests.name: Relocation Test (Jobe's)
special_tests.description: Apply posterior force to humeral head during apprehension test. Relief of apprehension is positive.

special_tests.name: Anterior Release Test
special_tests.description: Remove posterior pressure during relocation test. Return of apprehension is positive.

name: SLAP Lesion
description: Superior labrum anterior to posterior tear, commonly at the biceps anchor, seen in overhead athletes.
key_findings: Deep shoulder pain, painful clicking, pain with overhead activity and cross-body movements. Biceps instability in Type II.
diagnostic_tips: O'Brien's test is the most commonly used but low specificity. Speed's test tests biceps tendon. MRI arthrogram is gold standard. Common in baseball pitchers.

interventions: Rotator cuff and scapular stabilisation exercise

special_tests.name: O'Brien's Test (Active Compression)
special_tests.description: 90° flexion, 15° adduction, full IR — resist downward force. Pain/click in shoulder (not AC joint) is positive.

special_tests.name: Speed's Test
special_tests.description: Resist forward flexion with elbow extended and forearm supinated. Pain at bicipital groove is positive.

special_tests.name: Biceps Load Test II
special_tests.description: Elbow at 120° flexion, forearm supinated — resist elbow flexion. Pain at shoulder is positive for SLAP.

name: AC Joint Sprain/Separation
description: Injury to the acromioclavicular ligament and joint, graded I-VI by Rockwood classification.
causes: Direct fall on shoulder (point of shoulder)
key_findings: Point tenderness at AC joint, visible step deformity (Grade III+), positive cross-body adduction test, pain with horizontal adduction.
diagnostic_tips: Stress X-ray (weighted views) for grading. Palpate AC joint directly — exquisitely tender. Step deformity present in Grade III+. Grades I-III managed conservatively.

interventions: Progressive rotator cuff and deltoid strengthening
AC joint reconstruction (hook plate, anatomic repair)

special_tests.name: Cross-Body Adduction Test
special_tests.description: Passively adduct arm horizontally across chest. Pain at AC joint is positive.

special_tests.name: AC Joint Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation of AC joint. Point tenderness is highly suggestive.

special_tests.name: O'Brien's Test (AC variant)
special_tests.description: Pain at AC joint (not deep shoulder) during O'Brien's indicates AC pathology.

name: Biceps Tendinopathy (Long Head)
description: Tendinopathy or tenosynovitis of the long head of biceps tendon in the bicipital groove.
key_findings: Anterior shoulder pain in bicipital groove, tenderness on palpation of groove, pain with resisted supination and elbow flexion.
diagnostic_tips: Palpate bicipital groove with arm in 10° IR. Yergason's and Speed's tests implicate biceps. Often secondary to rotator cuff or AC pathology. USS shows tendon thickening or tenosynovitis.

special_tests.name: Speed's Test
special_tests.description: Resist shoulder forward flexion with elbow extended and forearm supinated. Bicipital groove pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Yergason's Test
special_tests.description: Resist forearm supination with elbow at 90° flexion. Bicipital groove pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Bicipital Groove Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate groove with arm in 10° IR. Point tenderness is positive.

name: Multidirectional Shoulder Instability (MDI)
description: Symptomatic glenohumeral instability in multiple directions (anterior, posterior, inferior) due to capsular laxity.
key_findings: Bilateral laxity, sulcus sign, positive load-and-shift test in multiple directions, feeling of 'giving way' with daily activities.
diagnostic_tips: Sulcus sign is hallmark (>1cm drop). Check Beighton score for systemic hypermobility. Distinguish from unidirectional traumatic instability. Avoid surgery until 6+ months rehab.

special_tests.name: Sulcus Sign
special_tests.description: Traction applied to arm at side. Inferior subluxation creating sulcus >1cm is positive for MDI.

special_tests.name: Load and Shift Test
special_tests.description: Load humeral head into glenoid, translate anteriorly and posteriorly. Grade laxity in each direction.

special_tests.name: Gagey Hyperabduction Test
special_tests.description: Passive abduction with scapula stabilised. >105° indicates inferior capsule laxity.

name: Calcific Tendinitis Shoulder
description: Calcium hydroxyapatite deposits within the rotator cuff tendons causing acute or chronic shoulder pain.
key_findings: Often acute severe pain (reabsorptive phase), X-ray calcium deposits in supraspinatus (most common). May be incidental finding.
diagnostic_tips: X-ray is diagnostic (dense calcium deposit). USS guides needling. Distinguish acute (formative) from reabsorptive phase — reabsorptive is more painful but better prognosis. Calcification may disappear spontaneously.

interventions: Arthroscopic calcium deposit removal if failed conservative 12+ months

special_tests.name: Impingement Tests (Hawkins-Kennedy, Neer)
special_tests.description: May be positive due to calcium deposit causing mechanical impingement in subacromial space.

special_tests.name: X-ray (AP, outlet, axillary views)
special_tests.description: Diagnostic: dense calcium deposit in supraspinatus tendon visible on plain X-ray.

special_tests.name: Ultrasound
special_tests.description: Characterises calcium type, guides barbotage. Hyperechoic focus with acoustic shadow.

name: Scapular Dyskinesis
description: Altered position or movement of the scapula contributing to shoulder impingement and dysfunction.
key_findings: Winging, excessive elevation or protraction during arm elevation. Reduced upward rotation. Associated with shoulder impingement.
diagnostic_tips: Observe scapular kinematics during arm elevation (anterior, posterior views). Wall push-up test for serratus weakness. Distinguish medial winging (serratus) from lateral winging (trapezius). Not always symptomatic.

interventions: Serratus anterior activation (wall slides, push-up plus)
Lower trapezius strengthening (prone Y, T, W)

special_tests.name: Scapular Assistance Test
special_tests.description: Manually assist scapular upward rotation during abduction. Improved symptoms indicates scapular contribution.

special_tests.name: Scapular Retraction Test
special_tests.description: Manually retract and depress scapula, re-test impingement tests. Improvement indicates scapular dyskinesis.

special_tests.name: Wall Push-Up Test
special_tests.description: Standard push-up against wall. Medial scapular winging indicates serratus anterior weakness.

name: Posterior Shoulder Instability
description: Posterior subluxation or dislocation of the glenohumeral joint, less common than anterior instability.
causes: Axial load with arm flexed and IR (seizure, fall)
key_findings: Posterior shoulder pain and apprehension with forward flexion, adduction, and IR. Positive posterior load-shift test.
diagnostic_tips: Often missed — suspect with posterior pain during bench press or push-up. Posterior apprehension test. Axillary X-ray to rule out posterior dislocation. MRI for reverse Bankart or Kim lesion.

special_tests.name: Posterior Apprehension/Jerk Test
special_tests.description: Axial load through flexed, adducted, IR shoulder. Posterior apprehension or jerk indicates posterior instability.

special_tests.name: Kim Test
special_tests.description: 90° ABD, axial load and inferior force while adducting. Posterior-inferior clunk/pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Posterior Drawer Test
special_tests.description: Stabilise scapula, push humeral head posteriorly. Increased translation is positive.

name: Shoulder OA (Glenohumeral Osteoarthritis)
description: Degenerative joint disease of the glenohumeral joint with cartilage loss and joint space narrowing.
key_findings: Global stiffness (different from capsular pattern), crepitus, pain at end range, X-ray joint space narrowing and osteophytes.
diagnostic_tips: X-ray (AP, axillary) is diagnostic. Crepitus distinguishes from soft tissue causes. Stiffness is global but differs from frozen shoulder by preserved ER early. Refer for joint replacement if severe.

interventions: Total shoulder arthroplasty (TSA) for primary OA
Reverse shoulder arthroplasty (RSA) if cuff deficient

special_tests.name: Crepitus Assessment
special_tests.description: Palpate glenohumeral joint during passive ROM. Crepitus with global restriction indicates OA.

special_tests.name: X-ray (AP Weight-Bearing)
special_tests.description: Joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, osteophytes confirm OA.

special_tests.name: Passive ROM Assessment
special_tests.description: Global restriction with hard end-feel (different from capsular pattern of frozen shoulder).

name: Pectoralis Major Strain/Rupture
description: Partial or complete rupture of the pectoralis major muscle, typically at the tendinous insertion on the humerus.
key_findings: Acute pain and 'pop' during bench press, ecchymosis in axilla/anterior chest, visible defect in pec contour, weakness with adduction/IR.
diagnostic_tips: Visible and palpable defect in the anterior axillary fold is diagnostic. USS or MRI for grade assessment. Complete ruptures (Grade III) require surgical repair in athletes. Early recognition critical.

interventions: Primary repair within 3 months for best outcomes

special_tests.name: Resisted Adduction/IR Test
special_tests.description: Resist horizontal adduction and IR at 60° ABD. Weakness and pain indicates pec major involvement.

special_tests.name: Anterior Axillary Fold Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate anterior axillary fold during resisted IR. Gap or deformity indicates partial/complete rupture.

special_tests.name: Lift-Off Test
special_tests.description: Place hand on lower back, resist lifting hand off back. Weakness in IR may suggest posterior pec or subscapularis involvement.

name: Suprascapular Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular or spinoglenoid notch causing posterior shoulder pain and weakness.
causes: Ganglion cyst at spinoglenoid notch (overhead athletes)
key_findings: Posterior/lateral shoulder pain, infraspinatus (± supraspinatus) atrophy and weakness in ER. Insidious onset in volleyball/overhead athletes.
diagnostic_tips: Infraspinatus wasting visible posteriorly. ER weakness without impingement signs. MRI shows fatty atrophy and may reveal cyst. EMG/NCS confirms diagnosis.

interventions: Arthroscopic decompression/cyst excision if failed conservative

special_tests.name: ER Weakness Test
special_tests.description: Resisted ER in 0° and 90° ABD. Selective infraspinatus weakness suggests spinoglenoid notch compression.

special_tests.name: Suprascapular Nerve Palpation
special_tests.description: Pressure at suprascapular notch. Reproduction of posterior shoulder pain.

special_tests.name: Cross-Body Adduction + Trunk Rotation
special_tests.description: Adduct arm and rotate trunk. Stretches suprascapular nerve — reproduces symptoms.

name: Lateral Epicondylitis (Tennis Elbow)
description: Tendinopathy of the common extensor tendon origin (ECRB predominantly) at the lateral epicondyle.
key_findings: Tenderness over ECRB origin at lateral epicondyle. Pain with resisted wrist extension and gripping. Painful grip.
diagnostic_tips: Point tender at ECRB origin (anterior aspect of lateral epicondyle). Cozen's is most reliable. Exclude posterior interosseous nerve entrapment (radial tunnel syndrome) — tender over arcade of Frohse, not epicondyle.

interventions: Bracing for load management during activity

special_tests.name: Cozen's Test
special_tests.description: Pronate forearm, fist clenched, resist wrist extension. Pain at lateral epicondyle is positive.

special_tests.name: Mill's Test
special_tests.description: Fully flex wrist and pronate while extending elbow. Provokes pain at lateral epicondyle.

special_tests.name: Maudsley's Test
special_tests.description: Resist middle finger extension. Pain at lateral epicondyle implicates ECRB.

name: Medial Epicondylitis (Golfer's Elbow)
description: Tendinopathy of the common flexor-pronator tendon origin at the medial epicondyle.
key_findings: Medial epicondyle tenderness, pain with resisted wrist flexion and pronation, pain with grip.
diagnostic_tips: Palpate medial epicondyle directly and just distal. Distinguish from UCL sprain (valgus stress test), medial nerve entrapment (paresthesia), and ulnar nerve involvement (cubital tunnel).

special_tests.name: Medial Epicondyle Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation. Point tenderness at or just distal to medial epicondyle is positive.

special_tests.name: Resisted Wrist Flexion
special_tests.description: Resist wrist flexion. Pain at medial epicondyle is positive.

special_tests.name: Resisted Pronation Test
special_tests.description: Resist forearm pronation. Pain at medial epicondyle implicates pronator teres/FCR origin.

name: UCL Sprain / Valgus Instability
description: Injury to the ulnar collateral ligament (medial ligament complex) of the elbow, common in throwing athletes.
key_findings: Medial elbow pain with throwing (late cocking/early acceleration), medial laxity on valgus stress, milking maneuver positive.
diagnostic_tips: Valgus stress at 30° flexion is key (unlocks olecranon). MRI arthrogram is gold standard for ligament integrity. Assess ulnar nerve — 40% have ulnar nerve symptoms.

special_tests.name: Valgus Stress Test
special_tests.description: Apply valgus force at 30° flexion. Medial laxity or pain indicates UCL injury.

special_tests.name: Milking Maneuver
special_tests.description: Thumb pull test in flexion producing valgus torque. Pain/laxity is positive.

special_tests.name: Moving Valgus Stress Test
special_tests.description: Apply valgus force throughout flexion/extension arc. Pain at 70-120° (shear zone) is positive.

name: Radial Tunnel Syndrome
description: Entrapment of the posterior interosseous nerve (deep radial nerve) in the radial tunnel, causing lateral elbow and forearm pain.
key_findings: Aching lateral elbow/proximal forearm pain, tenderness 4-5cm distal to lateral epicondyle (at arcade of Frohse), no neurological signs (distinguishes from PIN palsy).
diagnostic_tips: Tenderness is distal to lateral epicondyle (not at epicondyle as in tennis elbow). Middle finger extension test is positive (as in tennis elbow) — distinguish by tenderness location. Often misdiagnosed as resistant tennis elbow.

interventions: Radial tunnel decompression if failed 3-6 months
Decompression at fibrous arcade, radial recurrent vessels, ECRB edge

special_tests.name: Radial Tunnel Palpation (Arcade of Frohse)
special_tests.description: Tender over radial tunnel, 4-5cm distal to lateral epicondyle. Distinguishes from tennis elbow.

special_tests.name: Middle Finger Extension Stress Test
special_tests.description: Resist middle finger extension. Pain in radial tunnel (not at epicondyle) implicates radial tunnel syndrome.

special_tests.name: Resisted Supination Test
special_tests.description: Resist forearm supination at 90° elbow flexion. Pain in proximal forearm is positive.

name: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome (Ulnar Nerve)
description: Compression or traction of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel (posterior medial elbow), causing medial elbow pain and ulnar paresthesia.
key_findings: Paresthesia in ring and little fingers, medial elbow pain, positive Tinel's at cubital tunnel, weakness in intrinsics (late), Wartenberg's sign.
diagnostic_tips: Elbow flexion test is very sensitive. Distinguish from Guyon's canal (wrist) compression — cubital causes dorsal hand sensory changes, Guyon's does not. NCE/EMG confirms and grades severity.

interventions: Medial epicondylectomy or ulnar nerve transposition

special_tests.name: Elbow Flexion Test
special_tests.description: Fully flex elbow, extend wrist, shoulder elevated for 3 minutes. Ulnar paresthesia is positive.

special_tests.name: Tinel's Sign at Cubital Tunnel
special_tests.description: Tap over ulnar nerve behind medial epicondyle. Tingling into ring/little finger is positive.

special_tests.name: Froment's Sign
special_tests.description: Hold paper between thumb and index. Uses FPL not adductor pollicis (ulnar weakness). Thumb IP flexes.

name: Elbow OA / Loose Bodies
description: Degenerative joint disease of the elbow with osteophytes, joint space narrowing, and possible loose bodies.
key_findings: Extension loss (full extension first to go), painful terminal extension and flexion, crepitus, locking if loose bodies present.
diagnostic_tips: Extension loss is characteristic. X-ray for osteophytes and loose bodies. Locking suggests loose body. Distinguish from posterior impingement (soft tissue) and osteochondritis dissecans in young throwers.

interventions: Arthroscopic debridement and loose body removal

special_tests.name: Elbow Extension Test
special_tests.description: Full passive extension. Loss of extension with hard/firm end-feel suggests OA or posterior impingement.

special_tests.name: Crepitus Test
special_tests.description: Circumduction of elbow. Crepitus with restriction indicates intra-articular pathology.

special_tests.name: X-ray Assessment
special_tests.description: AP and lateral views show joint space narrowing, osteophytes at coronoid/olecranon, loose bodies.

name: Biceps Tendon Rupture (Distal)
description: Complete or partial rupture of the distal biceps tendon at its insertion on the radial tuberosity.
key_findings: Audible/felt pop in antecubital fossa, ecchymosis, visible retraction of biceps (Popeye sign), weakness in supination > elbow flexion.
diagnostic_tips: Supination weakness is greater than flexion weakness (diagnostic clue). Hook test: try to hook finger behind distal biceps tendon — absent in complete rupture. MRI for partial tears.

interventions: Full return to heavy work 4-6 months

special_tests.name: Hook Test
special_tests.description: Attempt to hook index finger behind distal biceps tendon in antecubital fossa. Absent cord indicates complete rupture.

special_tests.name: Biceps Squeeze Test
special_tests.description: Squeeze biceps muscle belly. No distal movement of forearm (supination) indicates distal rupture.

special_tests.name: Passive Forearm Pronation Gap Test
special_tests.description: Passively pronate relaxed forearm. Absent resistance indicates distal biceps rupture.

name: Olecranon Bursitis
description: Inflammation and fluid accumulation in the olecranon bursa, presenting as a posterior elbow swelling.
key_findings: Posterior elbow soft fluctuant swelling, usually non-tender unless septic/acute. Limited elbow flexion by swelling. Warm/red if septic.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish septic from non-septic: septic presents with erythema, warmth, fever, systemic symptoms. Aspirate for cell count and culture if septic suspected. Gout: urate crystals on aspiration.

special_tests.name: Bursa Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate posterior olecranon. Fluctuant soft swelling is positive. Assess for warmth, tenderness.

special_tests.name: Aspiration (Diagnostic)
special_tests.description: Aspirate bursal fluid. Cell count >50,000/mm3, positive culture indicates septic bursitis.

special_tests.name: Elbow Flexion ROM
special_tests.description: Full flexion assessment. Swelling-limited flexion without joint stiffness suggests bursal (not intra-articular) swelling.

name: Posterior Interosseous Nerve Palsy (PIN Palsy)
description: Motor palsy of the posterior interosseous nerve (deep branch of radial nerve) causing wrist/finger extension weakness without sensory loss.
causes: Nerve entrapment at arcade of Frohse
key_findings: Weakness of wrist extension (radially deviated), finger MCP extension weakness, thumb extension weakness. No sensory deficit (pure motor nerve).
diagnostic_tips: Pure motor deficit — no sensory loss distinguishes from radial nerve palsy. Wrist extends radially (ECRL intact — radial nerve proper). EMG/NCS for localisation.

special_tests.name: Wrist Extension (Radial Deviation)
special_tests.description: Wrist extends radially (ECRL intact). Weakness of ulnar wrist extension and finger extension confirms PIN palsy.

special_tests.name: Finger MCP Extension Test
special_tests.description: Request MCP extension of all fingers. Weakness without sensory deficit indicates PIN.

special_tests.name: EMG/NCS
special_tests.description: Electromyography shows denervation in PIN-innervated muscles. Confirms diagnosis and level.

name: Osteochondritis Dissecans (OCD) — Elbow
description: Avascular necrosis of subchondral bone at the capitellum, common in young overhead athletes.
key_findings: Lateral elbow pain in young athlete, reduced extension, pain with forearm rotation and compression. Locking if fragment detaches.
diagnostic_tips: Suspect OCD in any young overhead athlete with lateral elbow pain. X-ray may be negative early — MRI or CT arthrogram for staging. Stable vs. unstable lesion determines management.

special_tests.name: Radiocapitellar Compression Test
special_tests.description: Axial compression through extended elbow with forearm rotation. Lateral elbow pain suggests capitellar OCD.

special_tests.name: X-ray (AP, Lateral, Oblique)
special_tests.description: Subchondral lucency at capitellum. May be negative early — MRI is more sensitive.

special_tests.name: MRI Assessment
special_tests.description: Gold standard for staging. T2 signal change, fluid deep to fragment (unstable). Bone oedema.

name: Pronator Teres Syndrome
description: Compression of the median nerve between the two heads of pronator teres, causing forearm pain and median paresthesia.
key_findings: Proximal forearm aching, median nerve paresthesia (thumb, index, middle, lateral ring), worsened by resisted pronation. No thenar wasting (distinguishes from CTS in early stages).
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from carpal tunnel: pronator syndrome has positive Tinel's at forearm, symptoms worsened by prolonged elbow activity, no nocturnal symptoms. Resisted pronation is provocative.

interventions: Soft tissue therapy to pronator teres
Decompression of median nerve in proximal forearm
Release of pronator teres and lacertus fibrosus

special_tests.name: Pronator Teres Compression Test
special_tests.description: Firm pressure over pronator teres with forearm supinated. Paresthesia in median distribution is positive.

special_tests.name: Resisted Pronation Test
special_tests.description: Resist forearm pronation at 90° elbow flexion. Median forearm pain implicates pronator teres.

special_tests.name: Tinel's Sign (Proximal Forearm)
special_tests.description: Tap over pronator teres. Tingling in median distribution distal to tap site is positive.

name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
description: Compression of the median nerve as it passes through the carpal tunnel at the wrist.
key_findings: Paresthesia in thumb, index, middle, and lateral half of ring finger. Nocturnal symptoms, thenar wasting in severe cases.
diagnostic_tips: Nocturnal symptoms are hallmark. Thenar wasting indicates severe/chronic compression. NCE/EMG for confirmation and grading. Distinguish from cervical radiculopathy (C6/C7) and pronator syndrome.

interventions: Carpal tunnel release surgery (open or endoscopic)

special_tests.name: Phalen's Test
special_tests.description: Hold wrists in full flexion for 60 seconds. Paresthesia in median distribution is positive.

special_tests.name: Tinel's Sign at Wrist
special_tests.description: Tap over carpal tunnel. Tingling in median distribution is positive.

special_tests.name: Durkan's Compression Test
special_tests.description: Apply direct pressure over carpal tunnel for 30 seconds. Symptom reproduction is positive.

name: De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
description: Stenosing tenosynovitis of the first dorsal compartment (APL and EPB tendons) at the radial styloid.
key_findings: Radial wrist pain and swelling at radial styloid, positive Finkelstein's test, tenderness over first dorsal compartment.
diagnostic_tips: Finkelstein's test is hallmark (high sensitivity). Distinguish from intersection syndrome (pain more proximal, 4-6cm from Lister's tubercle), and basilar thumb OA (grinding at CMC1).

interventions: Release both APL and EPB subcompartments

special_tests.name: Finkelstein's Test
special_tests.description: Ulnar deviate wrist with thumb tucked in fist. Sharp radial pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Eichhoff's Test
special_tests.description: Similar to Finkelstein — patient wraps fingers around thumb, ulnar deviation. Radial pain is positive.

special_tests.name: First Dorsal Compartment Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation over APL/EPB tendons at radial styloid. Exquisite tenderness is positive.

name: Triangular Fibrocartilage Complex (TFCC) Tear
description: Injury to the TFCC (fibrocartilage and ligament complex on ulnar side of wrist), causing ulnar wrist pain and instability.
key_findings: Ulnar wrist pain, tenderness in fovea (between ulnar styloid and FCU), pain with forearm rotation and grip, may have click.
diagnostic_tips: Foveal tenderness is most reliable clinical sign (90%+ sensitivity). Press test: axial loading on hypothenar eminence reproduces pain. MRI arthrogram is gold standard. Distinguish from ECU tendinopathy and ulnar impaction.

special_tests.name: Foveal Sign (Ulnar Snuffbox)
special_tests.description: Palpation in fovea between FCU and ulnar styloid. Tenderness indicates TFCC foveal tear.

special_tests.name: TFCC Load Test (Press Test)
special_tests.description: Axial load on ulnar side (push up on palm). Pain indicates TFCC injury.

special_tests.name: Ulnar Grind Test
special_tests.description: Axial compression with ulnar deviation and rotation. Pain/click indicates TFCC or ulnocarpal pathology.

name: Scaphoid Fracture
description: Fracture of the scaphoid bone, the most common carpal bone fracture, with risk of avascular necrosis.
key_findings: Anatomical snuffbox tenderness, scaphoid tubercle tenderness, pain with axial thumb compression. Initial X-ray may be negative.
diagnostic_tips: Never dismiss snuffbox tenderness. X-ray negative in 10-15% initially — MRI or CT for definitive diagnosis. AVN risk at waist and proximal pole. Undisplaced: cast; displaced or proximal pole: surgery.

interventions: Grip and wrist strengthening from 8 weeks
Return to sport at union (3-6 months)

special_tests.name: Anatomical Snuffbox Tenderness
special_tests.description: Palpate snuffbox (between EPL and EPB). Tenderness has high sensitivity for scaphoid fracture.

special_tests.name: Scaphoid Tubercle Tenderness
special_tests.description: Palpate scaphoid tubercle on palmar surface. Combined with snuffbox tenderness increases accuracy.

special_tests.name: Axial Thumb Compression
special_tests.description: Apply axial load through extended thumb. Wrist pain indicates scaphoid involvement.

name: Trigger Finger (Stenosing Flexor Tenosynovitis)
description: Stenosis of the A1 pulley causing catching or locking of the flexor tendon during finger flexion.
key_findings: Painful triggering or locking of finger, nodule palpable at A1 pulley (MCP joint level), worse in morning.
diagnostic_tips: Palpate A1 pulley (palmar MCP crease). Click/nodule confirms diagnosis. Grade I-IV classification. Diabetes has high association. Corticosteroid injection has 60-90% success rates.

interventions: Corticosteroid injection at A1 pulley (1-2 injections)
Percutaneous or open A1 pulley release

special_tests.name: A1 Pulley Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate palmar MCP crease. Tender nodule with triggering on flexion/extension is diagnostic.

special_tests.name: Active Trigger Assessment
special_tests.description: Ask patient to make full fist. Observe for triggering or locking in any finger. Positive if mechanical catch present.

special_tests.name: Passive Extension Test
special_tests.description: If finger locked in flexion, attempt passive extension. Resistance/clunk confirms locked trigger finger.

name: Thumb CMC Osteoarthritis (Basilar Thumb OA)
description: Degenerative joint disease of the carpometacarpal joint of the thumb (trapezio-metacarpal joint).
key_findings: Radial wrist pain at base of thumb, grinding on circumduction (grind test), deformity (adduction contracture, hyperextension MCP), weakness in pinch.
diagnostic_tips: Grind test: axial load + rotation at CMC1. Distinguish from De Quervain (Finkelstein vs Grind). X-ray shows joint space narrowing and subluxation (thumb sign of CMC OA).

special_tests.name: Grind Test (CMC)
special_tests.description: Axial compression and rotation at CMC1 joint. Crepitus/pain indicates CMC OA.

special_tests.name: Distraction Test
special_tests.description: Traction applied to first metacarpal. Relief of pain with traction suggests CMC OA.

special_tests.name: Shear Test
special_tests.description: Stabilise trapezium, translate metacarpal. Pain/instability confirms carpometacarpal involvement.

name: Dupuytren's Contracture
description: Progressive fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia causing finger flexion contracture.
key_findings: Palmar nodules and cords, progressive flexion contracture of MCP and PIP joints (ring and little finger most common), unable to fully extend fingers.
diagnostic_tips: Tabletop test: inability to place palm flat indicates contracture. MCP > 30° and/or PIP > 0° are surgical thresholds. Distinguish from trigger finger and Camptodactyly. Diathesis predicts recurrence.

special_tests.name: Tabletop Test
special_tests.description: Place palm flat on table. Inability to achieve full contact indicates contracture.

special_tests.name: MCP/PIP Goniometry
special_tests.description: Measure passive extension deficit at MCP and PIP. >30° at MCP or any PIP deficit indicates surgical threshold.

special_tests.name: Palmar Cord Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate palmar fascia for cord or nodule. Indicates Dupuytren's disease.

name: Hip Labral Tear
description: Tear of the acetabular labrum, often associated with femoroacetabular impingement (FAI).
key_findings: Deep groin pain, clicking or giving way, positive FADIR test, C-sign (gripping hip with thumb and finger).
diagnostic_tips: FADIR has high sensitivity but low specificity (many causes). C-sign (patient cups hip) suggests intra-articular pathology. MRI arthrogram is gold standard. Identify FAI morphology (Cam/Pincer) on plain X-ray.

interventions: Pincer rim trimming if Pincer FAI

special_tests.name: FADIR Test
special_tests.description: Flex hip to 90°, adduct and internally rotate. Groin pain/click is positive for labral pathology.

special_tests.name: FABER Test
special_tests.description: Flex, abduct, and externally rotate hip (figure-4). Groin pain indicates hip pathology.

special_tests.name: Posterior Impingement Test
special_tests.description: Extend and externally rotate hip. Posterior hip pain indicates posterior impingement.

name: Hip Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the hip with progressive cartilage loss and joint space narrowing.
key_findings: Groin/anterior hip pain, capsular pattern (ER > ABD > IR > Flexion), crepitus, morning stiffness, antalgic gait.
diagnostic_tips: Capsular pattern of restriction is hallmark. X-ray (weight-bearing AP pelvis): joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis. FABER and log roll test reproduce symptoms.

interventions: Exercise therapy (strengthening, aerobic, aquatic — all equivalent)
Total hip replacement (THR) for end-stage OA

special_tests.name: Log Roll Test
special_tests.description: Passive internal and external rotation of extended hip. Groin pain indicates intra-articular pathology.

special_tests.name: FABER Test
special_tests.description: Hip flexion, abduction, ER (figure-4). Groin pain or restricted range indicates hip OA.

special_tests.name: Hip IR Range (Supine)
special_tests.description: Passive IR in 90° flexion. <25° IR indicates significant hip pathology.

name: Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome (GTPS)
description: Tendinopathy ± bursitis of the gluteal tendons at the greater trochanter.
key_findings: Lateral hip pain, tender over greater trochanter, pain with single leg stance, lying on affected side. Positive FABER.
diagnostic_tips: Compressive load is the primary pain driver (not friction/movement). Avoid corticosteroid injections as first line — progressive exercise is superior at 12 months (LEAP trial). Palpate greater trochanter directly.

interventions: Avoid hip adduction (crossing legs, hand on hip)
Running gait retraining (reduce hip adduction angle)
Corticosteroid injection (faster short-term relief, inferior at 12 months)

special_tests.name: FABER Test (Lateral Hip Pain)
special_tests.description: Figure-4 position. Lateral hip pain (not groin) indicates gluteal tendinopathy/GTPS.

special_tests.name: Greater Trochanter Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation over greater trochanter and posterior facet. Point tenderness is positive.

special_tests.name: Single Leg Stance (30 sec)
special_tests.description: 30 seconds single leg stance on affected limb. Reproduction of lateral hip pain is positive.

name: Femoral Acetabular Impingement (FAI)
description: Abnormal contact between the femoral head-neck junction and acetabular rim during hip motion, causing pain and labral/cartilage damage.
causes: Repetitive hip loading in adolescent development
key_findings: Groin pain with flexion activities (sitting, cycling), positive FADIR (impingement test), reduced IR and flexion. Often in athletic population.
diagnostic_tips: Alpha angle >55° on X-ray/MRI (Cam). Cross-over sign on AP pelvis (Pincer). FADIR test is most sensitive clinical test. MRI arthrogram for labral/cartilage assessment. Not all morphology causes symptoms.

special_tests.name: FADIR (Impingement Test)
special_tests.description: 90° hip flexion, adduction, internal rotation. Groin pain is positive for Cam/Pincer FAI.

special_tests.name: Hip Internal Rotation (90° Flexion)
special_tests.description: Passive IR in 90° flexion. <20° IR suggests Cam FAI.

special_tests.name: Stinchfield Test (Resisted SLR)
special_tests.description: Resist hip flexion at 30-45°. Groin pain suggests intra-articular pathology.

name: Piriformis Syndrome
description: Compression of the sciatic nerve by the piriformis muscle causing buttock pain and radicular symptoms.
key_findings: Deep buttock pain, sciatic radiation (not from spine), tender piriformis, pain with prolonged sitting, positive FAIR test.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis of exclusion — rule out lumbar disc and SIJ pathology first. FAIR test (flexion, adduction, IR) stresses piriformis. Distinguish from lumbar radiculopathy (neurological signs, positive SLR, imaging).

interventions: Deep tissue massage/dry needling to piriformis
Botulinum toxin injection into piriformis (USS/EMG guided)

special_tests.name: FAIR Test
special_tests.description: Supine hip flexion, adduction, IR (FAIR position). Sciatic pain reproduction indicates piriformis syndrome.

special_tests.name: Piriformis Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate piriformis through posterior buttock or rectally. Point tenderness indicates piriformis involvement.

special_tests.name: Seated Piriformis Stretch
special_tests.description: Patient sits, places foot on contralateral knee. Lean forward. Sciatic reproduction is positive.

name: Hip Flexor Strain (Iliopsoas)
description: Strain or tear of the iliopsoas muscle-tendon unit, common in kicking and sprinting sports.
key_findings: Anterior hip/groin pain, tenderness over lesser trochanter, pain and weakness with resisted hip flexion.
diagnostic_tips: Point tenderness at lesser trochanter (iliopsoas insertion). Resisted hip flexion in 90° flexion is most provocative. USS for tendon continuity. Distinguish from hip labral tear, adductor strain, and femoral hernia.

interventions: Isometric hip flexion exercises within pain-free range
Progressive hip flexor strengthening (concentric then eccentric)

special_tests.name: Resisted Hip Flexion Test
special_tests.description: Resist hip flexion at 90° knee flexion. Groin/anterior hip pain indicates iliopsoas involvement.

special_tests.name: Thomas Test
special_tests.description: Supine hip flexion stretch. Anterior hip pain and limited hip extension indicates iliopsoas tightness/strain.

special_tests.name: Lesser Trochanter Palpation
special_tests.description: Deep palpation over lesser trochanter (medial thigh with hip ER). Point tenderness confirms iliopsoas insertion injury.

name: Adductor Strain (Groin Strain)
description: Muscle strain of the adductor longus (most common) or other hip adductor muscles.
key_findings: Medial groin pain, adductor tenderness (usually proximal), pain with resisted adduction.
diagnostic_tips: Palpate along adductor longus from pubic origin distally. Squeeze test (adduction against resistance) is highly sensitive. Distinguish from osteitis pubis (bilateral, pubic symphysis tenderness, X-ray changes) and inguinal hernia.

special_tests.name: Adductor Squeeze Test (0°)
special_tests.description: Squeeze examiner's fist between thighs with hips at 0°. Groin pain >7/10 indicates adductor strain.

special_tests.name: Adductor Squeeze Test (45°)
special_tests.description: Squeeze with hips at 45°. Most sensitive position for adductor longus.

special_tests.name: Resisted Hip Adduction
special_tests.description: Resist hip adduction in side-lying. Pain along adductor longus indicates strain.

name: Snapping Hip (Coxa Saltans)
description: Audible or palpable snapping around the hip joint — external (ITB over trochanter), internal (iliopsoas), or intra-articular (loose body).
causes: ITB snapping over greater trochanter (external type)
Iliopsoas snapping over iliopectineal eminence (internal type)
key_findings: Audible/palpable snap with hip movement. External: lateral; Internal: anterior; Intra-articular: deep pain with click.
diagnostic_tips: External type: painless snap laterally during hip flexion/extension. Internal type: snapping felt in anterior hip during hip extension from flexion. Dynamic USS can visualise tendon snapping. If painful and deep — rule out intra-articular pathology.

interventions: Corticosteroid injection to trochanteric bursa if painful
Treat underlying cause (labral repair, loose body removal)

special_tests.name: Ober's Test
special_tests.description: Side-lying ITB tightness test. Positive if hip remains abducted. Tight ITB associated with external coxa saltans.

special_tests.name: Dynamic Hip Circumduction
special_tests.description: Active hip circumduction in standing. Observe/palpate for snapping location.

special_tests.name: Ely's Test
special_tests.description: Prone knee flexion. Tight rectus femoris/TFL contributes to snapping hip.

name: Hip AVN (Avascular Necrosis)
description: Death of bone tissue in the femoral head due to disruption of blood supply, leading to collapse and joint destruction.
key_findings: Groin pain with weight-bearing and motion, early presentation similar to OA, eventual collapse causing severe hip OA.
diagnostic_tips: Early MRI can detect before X-ray changes (dark T1, bright T2 crescent sign). X-ray late changes: subchondral collapse, sclerosis. Risk stratification (ARCO staging) guides treatment. High clinical suspicion in steroid users with hip pain.

special_tests.name: Log Roll Test
special_tests.description: Passive internal and external rotation of extended hip. Pain indicates intra-articular pathology including AVN.

special_tests.name: MRI Hip (T1/T2)
special_tests.description: Hypointense T1 band sign, bone marrow oedema. Gold standard for early AVN detection.

special_tests.name: X-ray (AP and Frog-Leg)
special_tests.description: Late changes: crescent sign, subchondral collapse. Negative in early disease.

name: ACL Tear
description: Rupture of the anterior cruciate ligament, primary knee stabiliser for anterior translation and rotation.
causes: Valgus collapse with knee near extension
key_findings: Acute haemarthrosis, positive Lachman's test, positive pivot shift. Often associated meniscal/MCL injury.
diagnostic_tips: Lachman's test is most sensitive. Pivot shift most specific. Haemarthrosis at 6-24 hours strongly suggests ACL or PCL injury. MRI to confirm and assess concomitant injuries. Address bone bruises and concomitant meniscal tears in planning.

interventions: Phase 1 (0-12 weeks): ROM, quad activation, gait

special_tests.name: Lachman's Test
special_tests.description: Knee at 20-30° flexion, stabilise femur, translate tibia anteriorly. Soft/absent endpoint indicates ACL rupture.

special_tests.name: Anterior Drawer Test
special_tests.description: Knee at 90°, pull tibia anteriorly. Increased translation indicates ACL injury.

special_tests.name: Pivot Shift Test
special_tests.description: Valgus and IR while extending knee. Clunk indicates ACL insufficiency.

name: PCL Tear
description: Rupture of the posterior cruciate ligament, primary restraint to posterior tibial translation.
causes: Direct blow to anterior tibia (dashboard injury)
Fall on flexed knee with plantar-flexed foot
key_findings: Posterior knee pain, positive posterior drawer, posterior sag sign (gravity sag of tibia). Often less symptomatic than ACL.
diagnostic_tips: Sag sign (gravity posterior displacement of tibia in 90° flexion) is pathognomonic. Posterior drawer must start from neutral (not from sagged position). Most PCL tears managed conservatively.

interventions: Knee brace (PCL brace preventing tibial sag)
PCL reconstruction (transtibial or tibial inlay technique)

special_tests.name: Posterior Sag Sign
special_tests.description: Supine, 90° hip and knee flexion. Posterior tibial sag (gravity sag) indicates PCL disruption.

special_tests.name: Posterior Drawer Test
special_tests.description: 90° knee flexion, push tibia posteriorly. Increased displacement indicates PCL injury.

special_tests.name: Quadriceps Active Test
special_tests.description: 90° knee flexion with foot stabilised. Quad contraction causes anterior tibial shift (from sagged position). Positive indicates PCL.

name: Meniscus Tear
description: Tear of medial or lateral meniscus due to acute injury or degenerative change.
key_findings: Joint line tenderness, McMurray's positive, pain with deep squat. Medial more common.
diagnostic_tips: Joint line palpation is simple and reliable. Combine tests for better accuracy. MRI gold standard. Degenerative tears in >45 yr old: exercise = surgery at 2 years. Traumatic tears in young: consider surgery if mechanical symptoms.

interventions: Progressive rehabilitation to return to sport

special_tests.name: McMurray's Test
special_tests.description: Flex knee, apply varus/valgus with IR/ER, extend. Click/pain indicates meniscal tear.

special_tests.name: Thessaly Test
special_tests.description: Single leg stance at 20° flexion, rotate torso. Pain or locking indicates meniscal involvement.

special_tests.name: Joint Line Palpation
special_tests.description: Medial or lateral joint line tenderness. Point tenderness is highly suggestive.

name: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome (PFPS)
description: Anterior knee pain related to patellar tracking dysfunction and overload of the patellofemoral joint.
key_findings: Anterior knee pain aggravated by stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting (theatre sign). Positive Clarke's sign.
diagnostic_tips: Theatre sign (pain with prolonged sitting, relieved by extension) is hallmark. Assess hip abductor strength. Patellar tilt test for lateral retinaculum tightness. Exclude medial plica syndrome and Hoffa's fat pad impingement.

interventions: Load management (reduce squatting, stairs, running temporarily)
Combined hip + quad strengthening (superior to either alone)

special_tests.name: Clarke's Sign (Patellar Grind)
special_tests.description: Press patella distally, patient contracts quads. Pain indicates patellofemoral involvement.

special_tests.name: Step-Down Test
special_tests.description: Single leg squat from step. Knee valgus and anterior pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Patellar Tilt Test
special_tests.description: Attempt to lift lateral border of patella. Less than neutral tilt indicates tight lateral retinaculum.

name: MCL Sprain
description: Injury to the medial collateral ligament from valgus stress to the knee.
key_findings: Medial knee pain, valgus stress laxity (Grade II/III), tenderness along MCL, swelling (less than ACL tear).
diagnostic_tips: Grade I/II: isolated MCL — managed conservatively. Grade III with large laxity: check for ACL/PCL involvement (O'Donoghue's unhappy triad: MCL + ACL + Medial Meniscus). Stress X-ray at 30° flexion for true laxity measurement.

interventions: Consider surgical repair if combined instability

special_tests.name: Valgus Stress Test (0° and 30°)
special_tests.description: Apply valgus force at 0° (PCL/post capsule) and 30° (isolated MCL). Grade laxity I-III.

special_tests.name: MCL Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate medial femoral condyle, joint line, and medial tibial flare. Locate grade of injury.

special_tests.name: Anteromedial Rotary Instability Test
special_tests.description: Valgus + IR stress. Anteromedial opening indicates MCL + ACL involvement.

name: LCL / Posterolateral Corner Injury
description: Injury to the lateral collateral ligament and posterolateral corner structures (popliteus, popliteofibular ligament).
key_findings: Lateral knee pain, varus laxity, positive dial test, posterolateral rotary instability.
diagnostic_tips: PLC injuries frequently combined with ACL/PCL. Dial test at 30° vs 90° distinguishes isolated PLC (positive at 30° only) from combined PLC + PCL (positive at both). Often missed acutely.

special_tests.name: Varus Stress Test (0° and 30°)
special_tests.description: Apply varus force at 0° and 30°. Laxity at 30° indicates LCL injury; at 0° suggests capsular/PLC involvement.

special_tests.name: Dial Test
special_tests.description: External tibial rotation at 30° and 90° knee flexion (prone). >10° difference at 30° only = isolated PLC; both = PCL + PLC.

special_tests.name: Posterolateral Drawer Test
special_tests.description: Posterior draw with ER. Posterolateral rotation indicates PLC injury.

name: Patellar Tendinopathy (Jumper's Knee)
description: Tendinopathy of the patellar tendon at its insertion to the inferior patellar pole, common in jumping athletes.
key_findings: Inferior patellar pole pain, tender patellar tendon on palpation, pain with jumping/squatting, VISA-P score.
diagnostic_tips: Tenderness at inferior patellar pole (not superior — Sinding-Larsen in adolescents). USS: hypoechoic tendon, neovascularisation. Compressive loads make it worse — avoid full squat angle with high load. VISA-P for monitoring.

interventions: Isometric leg press at 45°: 4 x 45 sec at 70% MVIC — provides immediate analgesia
Leg press, hack squat, or squat with slow tempo
3 x 15 reps, progress to 4 x 6 reps over 12 weeks

special_tests.name: Patellar Tendon Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate inferior patellar pole with knee extended. Tenderness is diagnostic.

special_tests.name: Single Leg Decline Squat
special_tests.description: Single leg squat on 25° decline board. Pain <3 indicates mild tendinopathy; >6 is severe.

special_tests.name: VISA-P Score
special_tests.description: Victorian Institute of Sport Assessment — Patella questionnaire. <80 indicates clinically significant tendinopathy.

name: Iliotibial Band Syndrome (ITBS)
description: Overuse injury causing lateral knee pain due to compression of the ITB over the lateral femoral epicondyle.
key_findings: Lateral knee pain at lateral femoral epicondyle, worsened by running downhill, pain at 30° knee flexion (impingement zone), Noble compression test positive.
diagnostic_tips: Pain typically starts at a consistent distance/time into run. Noble compression test at 30° flexion is key. Assess hip abductor strength. Address training load before biomechanics.

interventions: Gait retraining (increase step rate, reduce hip adduction)

special_tests.name: Noble Compression Test
special_tests.description: Compress ITB over lateral femoral condyle with knee at 30° flexion. Lateral knee pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Ober's Test
special_tests.description: Side-lying passive hip adduction with flexed knee. Inability to adduct indicates ITB tightness.

special_tests.name: Renne Test
special_tests.description: Weight-bearing knee bend to 30°. Lateral femoral condyle pain is positive for ITBS.

name: Knee Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the knee with progressive cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and joint space narrowing.
key_findings: Knee pain with activity, morning stiffness <30 mins, crepitus, joint line tenderness, reduced ROM, bony enlargement.
diagnostic_tips: Clinical diagnosis in patients >45 with activity-related pain and morning stiffness <30 mins. X-ray standing AP: joint space narrowing (medial most common), osteophytes. BMI modification is critical.

interventions: Exercise therapy (strengthening + aerobic — all forms effective)
Weight management (5% loss = significant pain reduction)
Patient education (OA not caused by exercise)
Total knee replacement (TKR) for end-stage OA

special_tests.name: Clinical Criteria (ACR)
special_tests.description: Age >50, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, bony tenderness, bony enlargement. 3/5 = OA.

special_tests.name: Standing X-ray (AP Flexed)
special_tests.description: Standing AP: medial joint space narrowing. Rosenberg view (PA 45° flexion) more sensitive for cartilage loss.

special_tests.name: Joint Line Palpation
special_tests.description: Medial and lateral joint line palpation. Tenderness with crepitus indicates compartmental OA.

name: Pes Anserine Bursitis
description: Inflammation of the pes anserine bursa (deep to the conjoined tendons of sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus) at the medial tibia.
key_findings: Medial tibial pain 5-7cm below joint line, tender pes anserine bursa, pain with stair climbing, worse at night.
diagnostic_tips: Tenderness is below the joint line (distinguishes from MCL sprain and medial meniscus). Common in obese patients with OA. USS confirms bursal fluid. Corticosteroid injection is effective.

special_tests.name: Pes Anserine Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate medial tibia, 3-5cm distal to joint line. Tenderness indicates pes anserine bursitis.

special_tests.name: Resisted Knee Flexion
special_tests.description: Resist knee flexion in prone. Pain at medial tibia may indicate pes anserine or hamstring tendon involvement.

special_tests.name: Stair Climbing Pain Provocation
special_tests.description: Stair descent reproduces medial tibial pain at pes anserine site.

name: Baker's Cyst (Popliteal Cyst)
description: Fluid-filled synovial cyst in the popliteal fossa, usually secondary to intra-articular pathology.
key_findings: Posterior knee fullness, tender popliteal mass, may reduce on knee extension, pain with squatting and stair climbing.
diagnostic_tips: Baker's cyst is almost always secondary — treat the primary cause (OA, meniscal tear, RA). USS confirms cyst. Do not confuse with DVT (USS/Doppler to rule out rupture mimicking DVT).

interventions: Corticosteroid injection into joint (reduces fluid production)
Arthroscopic cyst excision if failed conservative and large symptomatic cyst

special_tests.name: Posterior Popliteal Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate popliteal fossa in full extension. Smooth, fluctuant mass is positive.

special_tests.name: Foucher's Sign
special_tests.description: Mass reduces with full knee extension and becomes prominent with flexion.

special_tests.name: USS Popliteal Fossa
special_tests.description: Ultrasound confirms anechoic or hypoechoic cyst between medial head of gastrocnemius and semimembranosus.

name: Ankle Lateral Ligament Sprain
description: Injury to the lateral ankle ligament complex (ATFL most common, CFL), graded I-III.
key_findings: Lateral ankle pain and swelling, positive anterior drawer, ATFL and CFL tenderness.
diagnostic_tips: Ottawa Ankle Rules to exclude fracture (base 5th metatarsal, malleolar tenderness). Anterior drawer and talar tilt assess ATFL and CFL integrity. Proprioceptive deficits persist 12 months — key rehab target.

interventions: POLICE protocol (Protect, Optimal Load, Ice, Compression, Elevation)
Ankle taping or bracing for return to sport

special_tests.name: Anterior Drawer Test
special_tests.description: Draw calcaneus anteriorly at 10-15° plantar flexion. Increased laxity indicates ATFL tear.

special_tests.name: Talar Tilt Test
special_tests.description: Invert calcaneus. Increased inversion compared to uninvolved side indicates CFL.

special_tests.name: Ottawa Ankle Rules
special_tests.description: Bony tenderness posterior fibula/tibia, base 5th MT. Used to rule out fracture.

name: Plantar Fasciitis
description: Degenerative condition of the plantar fascia causing inferior heel pain, most common foot complaint.
key_findings: Inferior heel pain worst with first steps in morning, medial calcaneal tuberosity tenderness.
diagnostic_tips: First step pain in morning (post-static dyskinesia) is hallmark. Windlass test reproduces pain. Weight-bearing DF <10° is a key risk factor. Distinguish from calcaneal stress fracture (squeeze test), nerve entrapment.

interventions: High-load strengthening: calf raises on rolled towel (Rathleff Protocol)

special_tests.name: Windlass Test
special_tests.description: Extend great toe passively. Reproduces heel pain — indicates plantar fascia involvement.

special_tests.name: Calcaneal Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation of medial calcaneal tuberosity. Point tenderness is characteristic.

special_tests.name: Weight-Bearing Dorsiflexion
special_tests.description: Less than 10° WB ankle DF is risk factor. Assess with lunge test.

name: Achilles Tendinopathy
description: Tendinopathy of the Achilles tendon at the mid-portion (2-6cm proximal to insertion) or insertional.
key_findings: Mid-portion or insertional tendon pain, morning stiffness, tender tendon with Royal London Hospital test, VISA-A score.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish mid-portion (2-6cm from insertion) from insertional (at bone-tendon junction) — different loading protocols needed. Corticosteroid injections are CONTRAINDICATED (rupture risk). USS: hypoechoic tendon, neovascularisation.

interventions: Eccentric heel drops on step (3x15 twice daily) — Alfredson protocol
OR Heavy slow resistance (HSR) — comparable outcomes, better adherence
Isometric calf holds for analgesia (in season)
Avoid compression loading (no heel drop below step level initially)

special_tests.name: VISA-A Score
special_tests.description: Victorian Institute of Sport Assessment — Achilles questionnaire. <80 indicates clinically significant tendinopathy.

special_tests.name: Royal London Hospital Test
special_tests.description: Palpate Achilles at painful point. Dorsiflexion reduces palpable pain in mid-portion tendinopathy.

special_tests.name: Single Leg Heel Raise
special_tests.description: Assess for pain and endurance. <25 reps compared to opposite limb indicates calf/tendon dysfunction.

name: Chronic Ankle Instability (CAI)
description: Recurrent ankle giving way and instability following repeated lateral ankle sprains, due to mechanical and neuromuscular deficits.
key_findings: Feeling of giving way, repeated sprains, positive anterior drawer (mechanical laxity), peroneal weakness.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish mechanical laxity (positive drawer) from functional instability (normal laxity, proprioceptive deficit). Cumberland Ankle Instability Tool (CAIT) for classification. Balance assessment reveals deficits.

special_tests.name: CAIT Questionnaire
special_tests.description: Cumberland Ankle Instability Tool. Score <27/30 indicates clinically significant CAI.

special_tests.name: Anterior Drawer (Stress X-ray)
special_tests.description: Bilateral comparison. >10mm side-to-side difference indicates mechanical laxity.

special_tests.name: Y-Balance Test (Anterior Reach)
special_tests.description: Single leg balance with reach. Asymmetry >4cm is risk factor for ankle injury recurrence.

name: Metatarsal Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue fracture of the metatarsal shaft due to repetitive loading, most common in 2nd and 3rd metatarsals.
key_findings: Localised metatarsal tenderness, pain with forefoot loading, percussion tenderness, tuning fork test.
diagnostic_tips: X-ray negative in first 2 weeks — MRI or bone scan for early diagnosis. 5th metatarsal base fracture: distinguish Jones fracture (zone II — poor blood supply, slower healing) from dancer's fracture (zone I, avulsion). Female Athlete Triad screening.

interventions: Non-weight-bearing / crutches (Jones fracture, Jones zone II)
Progressive weight-bearing based on pain resolution
Bone health assessment (Vitamin D, Calcium, RED-S screen)

special_tests.name: Metatarsal Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation along shaft. Focal tenderness is positive for stress fracture.

special_tests.name: Tuning Fork Test
special_tests.description: 128Hz tuning fork on shaft. Vibration reproduction of pain increases fracture suspicion.

special_tests.name: MRI Foot
special_tests.description: Gold standard for early stress fracture. T2 bone marrow oedema, periosteal reaction.

name: Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction (PTTD)
description: Degeneration and dysfunction of the posterior tibial tendon leading to acquired flat foot deformity.
key_findings: Medial ankle pain, 'too many toes' sign, inability to perform single leg heel raise, progressive flat foot deformity.
diagnostic_tips: Too many toes sign: viewing from behind, >2 toes visible laterally. Single leg heel raise failure is hallmark. Staging I-IV guides management. USS or MRI confirms tendon degeneration.

interventions: UCBL or custom orthotics (arch support, medial posting)

special_tests.name: Single Leg Heel Raise Test
special_tests.description: Stand on one leg, rise onto toes. Inability or pain indicates PTT insufficiency.

special_tests.name: Too Many Toes Sign
special_tests.description: View patient from behind. More than 2 toes visible on lateral side indicates hindfoot valgus and flat foot (PTTD).

special_tests.name: Medial Ankle Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate along PTT course from medial malleolus to navicular. Tenderness indicates PTTD.

name: Morton's Neuroma
description: Perineural fibrosis of the interdigital nerve, most commonly the third web space, causing burning forefoot pain.
key_findings: Burning/shooting pain in 3rd/4th toe web space, Mulder's click, relief with shoe removal.
diagnostic_tips: Mulder's sign: squeeze metatarsal heads laterally while pressing between metatarsal heads — click and pain are positive. Distinguish from metatarsal stress fracture (bone pain) and synovitis. USS confirms if >5mm neuroma.

interventions: Post-op walking in stiff-soled shoe 4-6 weeks

special_tests.name: Mulder's Sign
special_tests.description: Squeeze metatarsal heads + direct pressure in web space. Audible click with pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Interdigital Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct pressure in 3rd web space from plantar surface. Burning or shooting pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Web Space Compression
special_tests.description: Lateral metatarsal squeeze while compressing from dorsum. Pain in 3rd web space is positive.

name: Peroneal Tendon Pathology
description: Tendinopathy, subluxation, or tear of the peroneus brevis (most common) or longus tendons behind the lateral malleolus.
key_findings: Posterior lateral ankle pain, peroneal tendon tenderness, peroneal subluxation snap with ankle circumduction.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from lateral ankle ligament sprain: peroneal tenderness is posterior to fibula (not anterior). Dynamic USS shows subluxation. Longitudinal tears of peroneus brevis (split tear) seen on USS/MRI.

interventions: Conservative: bracing 6 weeks for acute subluxation
Surgical: peroneal groove deepening or superior peroneal retinaculum repair for chronic subluxation

special_tests.name: Peroneal Tendon Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate posterior to lateral malleolus along peroneal tendons. Tenderness indicates tendon pathology.

special_tests.name: Resisted Eversion Test
special_tests.description: Resist ankle eversion. Pain posterior to lateral malleolus indicates peroneal tendon pathology.

special_tests.name: Peroneal Subluxation Test
special_tests.description: Active dorsiflexion and eversion against resistance. Snap of tendon anterior to fibula confirms subluxation.

name: Cervical Radiculopathy
description: Compression or irritation of a cervical nerve root causing radicular symptoms into the upper extremity.
key_findings: Dermatomal arm pain/paresthesia, myotomal weakness, reflex changes. C6-C7 most common.
diagnostic_tips: C6: thumb paresthesia, biceps reflex reduced, brachialis weakness. C7: middle finger, triceps reflex, triceps weakness. Spurling's test reproduces symptoms. ULTT adds neural tension component. MRI for structural confirmation.

interventions: Cervical collar for short periods (comfort only, avoid dependence)
ACDR (cervical disc replacement) for single level

special_tests.name: Spurling's Test
special_tests.description: Extend + laterally flex neck toward symptomatic side, apply axial compression. Reproduces radicular symptoms.

special_tests.name: Shoulder Abduction Relief Test
special_tests.description: Abduct arm, place hand on head. Reduction in radicular symptoms indicates foraminal compression.

special_tests.name: Upper Limb Tension Test (ULTT)
special_tests.description: Progressive neural tension through shoulder depression, abduction, ER, elbow extension, wrist extension.

name: Cervical Myelopathy
description: Spinal cord compression in the cervical spine causing upper and lower motor neurone signs.
key_findings: Gait disturbance, hand dexterity loss, hyperreflexia, Hoffman's sign, clonus, bilateral symptoms, bowel/bladder changes.
diagnostic_tips: URGENT: myelopathy is a surgical condition. Do NOT perform cervical manipulation. Hoffman's sign, Babinski, ankle clonus, hyperreflexia, and gait ataxia are red flags. Refer urgently for MRI and neurosurgical review.

interventions: MRI cervical spine with myelopathy protocol

special_tests.name: Hoffman's Sign
special_tests.description: Flick middle finger distal IP. Thumb flexion/adduction indicates upper motor neurone lesion.

special_tests.name: Ankle Clonus
special_tests.description: Rapid dorsiflexion of ankle. Sustained rhythmic clonus indicates upper motor neurone pathology.

special_tests.name: Lhermitte's Sign
special_tests.description: Cervical flexion provokes electric shock sensation down spine/limbs. Indicates cord compression or irritation.

name: Cervical Disc Disease (Spondylosis)
description: Age-related degenerative changes of the cervical disc, facet joints, and ligaments causing neck pain and stiffness.
key_findings: Neck stiffness and pain, reduced ROM, referred pain to shoulder girdle, not necessarily radicular.
diagnostic_tips: Very common age-related finding. Image findings often do not correlate with symptoms — avoid over-medicalisation. Active management preferred. Distinguish from radiculopathy (arm pain, neurological signs) and myelopathy.

interventions: Upper trapezius and posterior cervical stretching

special_tests.name: Cervical ROM Assessment
special_tests.description: Goniometric or inclinometer measurement. Reduced extension and rotation are most common in cervical spondylosis.

special_tests.name: Axial Compression Test
special_tests.description: Apply axial load to cervical spine. Central neck pain indicates facet or disc pain.

special_tests.name: Cervical PA Assessment
special_tests.description: PA pressure on cervical spinous processes. Segmental stiffness and pain reproduction identifies dysfunctional segments.

name: Whiplash Associated Disorder (WAD)
description: Cervical injury from rapid acceleration-deceleration mechanism, classified by Quebec Task Force grades I-IV.
causes: Motor vehicle accident (rear-end collision most common)
key_findings: Neck pain and stiffness, headache, shoulder/arm referred pain, possible dizziness, photophobia. Quebec classification Grade I-IV.
diagnostic_tips: Psychosocial yellow flags (catastrophising, fear-avoidance) are strongest predictors of poor outcome. Early active management is key. Screen for vascular (vertebral artery) and neurological compromise. WAD Grade III/IV requires imaging.

interventions: Early active mobilisation (superior to collar immobilisation)

special_tests.name: Quebec WAD Classification
special_tests.description: Grade 0: no symptoms; I: pain no signs; II: MSK signs; III: neurological signs; IV: fracture/dislocation.

special_tests.name: Cervical ROM Measurement
special_tests.description: Reduced rotation and flexion common. Serial measurement tracks recovery.

special_tests.name: Cranio-Cervical Flexion Test (CCFT)
special_tests.description: Deep neck flexor endurance assessment. Reduced performance in WAD predicts poorer outcomes.

name: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome (TOS)
description: Compression of neurovascular structures (brachial plexus, subclavian vessels) in the thoracic outlet.
key_findings: Arm pain and paresthesia (usually ulnar distribution), muscle weakness, possible vascular symptoms (swelling, pallor, pulse change).
diagnostic_tips: Three types: neurogenic (most common, 95%), arterial (1%), venous (4%). Roos test is most useful. CXR for cervical rib. Distinguish from cervical radiculopathy (dermatomal pattern, Spurling's positive). Vascular TOS is urgent — USS/CT angiogram.

interventions: Postural correction and shoulder girdle strengthening

special_tests.name: Roos Test (EAST)
special_tests.description: Abduct arms 90°, ER, flex elbows 90°. Open/close fists for 3 minutes. Symptom reproduction is positive.

special_tests.name: Adson's Test
special_tests.description: Extend neck, rotate toward affected side, deep breath. Radial pulse diminution suggests subclavian compression.

special_tests.name: Wright's Test
special_tests.description: Hyperabduct arm above head. Pulse reduction or symptom reproduction indicates TOS.

name: Cervicogenic Headache
description: Referred head pain originating from the upper cervical spine (C1-C3), mimicking migraine or tension headache.
key_findings: Unilateral non-throbbing headache, neck pain/stiffness, headache triggered by neck movement or sustained posture. Reduced cervical ROM.
diagnostic_tips: IHS diagnostic criteria: unilateral, no side-shift, reduced cervical ROM, headache reproduced by PA pressure on upper cervical joints. Diagnostic cervical nerve block confirms diagnosis. Distinguish from migraine (side shift, autonomic features, nausea).

special_tests.name: C1-C3 PA Assessment
special_tests.description: Posteroanterior pressure on upper cervical joints (C1, C2, C3). Reproduction of headache is positive.

special_tests.name: Cervical Flexion Rotation Test
special_tests.description: Flex cervical spine, then rotate. Reduced rotation <32° at C1-C2 indicates cervicogenic headache.

special_tests.name: Suboccipital Tenderness
special_tests.description: Palpate suboccipital musculature. Reproduction of headache pattern is positive.

name: Cervical Facet Joint Pain
description: Pain originating from the zygapophyseal (facet) joints of the cervical spine, common cause of neck and referred pain.
key_findings: Neck pain with extension and rotation toward affected side, segmental tenderness on PA assessment, referred pain in characteristic patterns (C2/3: head; C3/4: neck/shoulder; C5/6: shoulder/arm).
diagnostic_tips: Gold standard diagnosis is medial branch block injection. Clinically: pain reproduced on PA testing of relevant segment. Distinguish from disc pain (flexion-predominant, referred into arm).

interventions: Radiofrequency ablation (if MBB effective — durable pain relief 12 months+)

special_tests.name: Cervical PA Pressure
special_tests.description: Posteroanterior pressure on articular pillars. Segmental pain reproduction indicates facet dysfunction.

special_tests.name: Extension-Rotation Test
special_tests.description: Extend and rotate toward symptomatic side. Ipsilateral pain indicates facet joint irritation.

special_tests.name: Segmental Mobility Testing
special_tests.description: Passive intervertebral motion testing (PIVM). Hypomobility with pain indicates facet dysfunction.

name: Atlanto-Axial Instability
description: Instability of the C1-C2 joint due to transverse ligament disruption or odontoid process abnormality.
key_findings: Neck pain, cervical stiffness, signs of cord compression if severe (myelopathy). Sharp neck pain with flexion.
diagnostic_tips: CONTRAINDICATION to cervical manipulation. Screen all RA patients and Down syndrome patients. VBI screening recommended. Neutral, flexion, extension X-ray to measure atlantodental interval (>3mm adult, >5mm child = abnormal). URGENT referral if cord signs.

interventions: Activity restriction (no contact sports, rotation extremes)
Physiotherapy: only stabilisation exercises, no manipulation

special_tests.name: Sharp-Purser Test
special_tests.description: Flex head, apply posterior pressure to forehead. Reduction of posterior head displacement (clunk) is positive.

special_tests.name: Atlantodental Interval (X-ray)
special_tests.description: >3mm (adult) or >5mm (child) in flexion indicates transverse ligament insufficiency.

special_tests.name: Alar Ligament Test
special_tests.description: Lateral flex head — ipsilateral restriction indicates intact alar ligament; excessive movement suggests tear.

name: Thoracic Spine Dysfunction
description: Segmental stiffness and pain of the thoracic spine affecting posture, respiration, and upper extremity function.
key_findings: Restricted thoracic rotation and extension, segmental stiffness on PA testing, referred pain to ribcage or shoulder.
diagnostic_tips: PA testing identifies stiff/painful segments. Thoracic kyphosis contributes to shoulder impingement. Referred pain to anterior chest can mimic cardiac pain (check cardiovascular first). Look for relationship between shoulder pain and thoracic restriction.

special_tests.name: PA Mobilisation Assessment
special_tests.description: Apply PA pressure to thoracic spinous processes. Stiffness/pain reproduction identifies dysfunctional segments.

special_tests.name: Thoracic Rotation Range
special_tests.description: Seated thoracic rotation. Less than 40° indicates significant restriction.

special_tests.name: Slump Test (Thoracic)
special_tests.description: Slump position focusing on thoracic symptoms. Distinguishes neural from musculoskeletal dysfunction.

name: Scheuermann's Disease
description: Juvenile osteochondrosis of the thoracic vertebrae causing structural kyphosis with vertebral wedging.
key_findings: Rigid thoracic kyphosis > 45°, vertebral wedging ≥5° in 3+ consecutive vertebrae on X-ray, reduced thoracic extension, back pain (not always present).
diagnostic_tips: Kyphosis that does not correct on extension distinguishes Scheuermann's from postural kyphosis. X-ray is diagnostic (Cobb angle, vertebral wedging). Brace if Cobb 45-75° in skeletally immature.

interventions: Thoracic brace (Milwaukee or TLSO) if Cobb 45-75°, adolescent

special_tests.name: Hyperextension Test
special_tests.description: Prone thoracic extension test. Structural kyphosis does not correct with extension (unlike postural).

special_tests.name: Cobb Angle Measurement (X-ray)
special_tests.description: Thoracic kyphosis Cobb angle measurement. >45° is significant; >75° is surgical threshold.

special_tests.name: Adams Forward Bend Test
special_tests.description: Forward trunk flexion. Thoracic rib hump asymmetry visible. Less prominent in Scheuermann's vs scoliosis.

name: Thoracic Compression Fracture
description: Vertebral body fracture with anterior column failure, most commonly osteoporotic or traumatic.
key_findings: Acute thoracic pain after minor trauma (osteoporotic), kyphotic deformity, tenderness over vertebra, height loss.
diagnostic_tips: Red flag: new back pain in older patient after minimal trauma. X-ray shows anterior wedging. MRI for acute vs chronic fracture (bone marrow oedema). Screen for underlying malignancy. DEXA scan for osteoporosis.

interventions: Percutaneous cement injection for refractory pain
Indicated if failed 4-6 weeks conservative management

special_tests.name: Vertebral Percussion Test
special_tests.description: Percussion over spinous processes. Pain at specific level indicates fracture or pathology.

special_tests.name: X-ray (AP + Lateral)
special_tests.description: Anterior vertebral wedging, loss of height, kyphotic angulation.

special_tests.name: MRI (STIR sequence)
special_tests.description: Bone marrow oedema indicates acute fracture. Rules out posterior element involvement and cord compression.

name: Costovertebral Joint Dysfunction
description: Pain arising from the costovertebral or costotransverse joint, presenting as thoracic and lateral chest pain.
key_findings: Unilateral thoracic pain, localised paravertebral tenderness at costotransverse joint, pain with deep breathing, referred intercostal pain.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from cardiac and pulmonary causes (always rule out first). Spring test over costotransverse joint reproduces pain. Rib torsion with rotation is provocative. Distinguish from thoracic disc (less common).

special_tests.name: Costovertebral Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate costotransverse junction. Point tenderness is positive for CVJ dysfunction.

special_tests.name: Rib Spring Test
special_tests.description: Anteroposterior pressure on posterior rib. Pain reproduction indicates costovertebral joint involvement.

special_tests.name: Thoracic Rotation Provocation
special_tests.description: Resisted rotation or passive rotation. Unilateral thoracic pain indicates facet or CVJ pathology.

name: Thoracic Disc Herniation
description: Posterolateral disc displacement in the thoracic spine, uncommon but potentially serious due to proximity to spinal cord.
