key_findings: Thoracic/mid-back pain, radicular pain wrapping around chest wall (dermatomal), possible myelopathy (lower limb signs) if central herniation.
diagnostic_tips: Thoracic disc herniation is uncommon (0.5% of disc herniations). Exclude other thoracic causes. MRI is essential. If lower limb signs, hyperreflexia, gait disturbance → urgent neurosurgical referral.

special_tests.name: Thoracic Slump Test
special_tests.description: Neural tension in slump position. Thoracic or radicular symptoms with neck flexion indicate neural involvement.

special_tests.name: Thoracic Extension Test
special_tests.description: Passive extension of thoracic spine. Centralisation of symptoms suggests discogenic pain with directional preference.

special_tests.name: Lower Limb Neurological Examination
special_tests.description: Reflexes, power, sensation in lower limbs. Hyperreflexia or Babinski indicates cord compromise — urgent referral.

name: Scoliosis (Adolescent Idiopathic)
description: 3D spinal deformity with lateral curvature and vertebral rotation, most common in adolescent females.
key_findings: Lateral spinal curvature, rib hump (Adam's forward bend), shoulder/hip asymmetry, positive Adam's test.
diagnostic_tips: Adams forward bend test: rib hump >7° on scoliometer warrants X-ray. Cobb angle: <10° normal, 10-25° monitor, 25-45° brace, >45-50° surgery (in growing spine). Assess Risser stage (skeletal maturity).

interventions: TLSO brace 18-23 hours/day until skeletal maturity

special_tests.name: Adams Forward Bend Test
special_tests.description: Forward trunk flexion. Rib or lumbar hump (rotation) is positive. Measure with scoliometer (>7° warrants X-ray).

special_tests.name: Cobb Angle (X-ray)
special_tests.description: Full spine standing AP X-ray. Cobb angle measured between end vertebrae. Guides management threshold.

special_tests.name: Scoliometer Assessment
special_tests.description: Angle of trunk rotation at maximum forward bend. >7° indicates clinically relevant scoliosis.

name: Non-Specific Low Back Pain
description: Back pain without specific structural cause, accounting for 90% of all low back pain presentations.
key_findings: Variable pain, often worse with sustained postures or movement. Normal neurological examination. Psychosocial factors often present.
diagnostic_tips: Avoid over-medicalisation and imaging without red flags. STarT Back Tool for risk stratification. Yellow flags (fear-avoidance, catastrophising) predict chronicity more than imaging. Active exercise is first-line.

interventions: Exercise therapy (type not superior — individualised is best)
Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for psychological factors
Manual therapy as adjunct to exercise

special_tests.name: STarT Back Tool
special_tests.description: 9-item questionnaire. Stratifies patients into low, medium, high risk for persistent disability.

special_tests.name: Fear-Avoidance Beliefs Questionnaire (FABQ)
special_tests.description: Assesses fear-avoidance beliefs about work and physical activity. High scores predict chronicity.

special_tests.name: Tampa Scale of Kinesiophobia
special_tests.description: Measures pain-related fear. High scores indicate need for graded exposure intervention.

name: Lumbar Disc Herniation
description: Posterolateral displacement of nucleus pulposus through annular fibers causing nerve root compression.
key_findings: Radicular leg pain dermatomal, positive SLR, neurological signs (reflexes, sensation, power).
diagnostic_tips: 90% resolve within 12 weeks. SLR highly sensitive in acute phase. Crossed SLR highly specific. L4/L5 and L5/S1 most common. Cauda equina symptoms (bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel) require urgent MRI and surgical referral.

interventions: Pain education and graded return to activity
Equivalent long-term outcomes to conservative at 1-2 years

special_tests.name: Straight Leg Raise (SLR)
special_tests.description: Passively raise extended leg. Radicular pain 30-70° indicates nerve root compression.

special_tests.name: Slump Test
special_tests.description: Seated slump with neck flexion. Radicular symptoms reproduced and relieved with neck extension.

special_tests.name: Crossed SLR
special_tests.description: SLR of uninvolved leg reproduces contralateral symptoms. High specificity for disc herniation.

name: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis
description: Narrowing of the lumbar spinal canal causing compression of neural elements and neurogenic claudication.
key_findings: Bilateral leg pain/heaviness/paresthesia with walking (neurogenic claudication), relieved by sitting/flexion, worse with extension.
diagnostic_tips: Classic: shopping cart sign (forward lean relieves symptoms). Distinguish from vascular claudication (peripheral pulses absent, ABI reduced, not relieved by leaning). MRI for canal diameter and stenosis grading.

interventions: Epidural steroid injection for radicular pain

special_tests.name: Two-Stage Treadmill Test
special_tests.description: Walk on flat vs inclined treadmill. Neurogenic claudication worse on flat (extension); vascular worse on incline.

special_tests.name: Extension Provocation
special_tests.description: Sustained lumbar extension. Bilateral leg symptoms indicate stenosis.

special_tests.name: Bicycle Test (van Gelderen)
special_tests.description: Cycle with trunk flexion vs extension. Flexion relieves, extension provokes neurogenic claudication.

name: Spondylolisthesis
description: Forward slippage of one vertebra on another, most commonly L4/L5 or L5/S1.
key_findings: Low back pain, hamstring tightness, palpable step deformity, possible radiculopathy. Extension and rotation provocative.
diagnostic_tips: Step deformity on palpation (lumbar spinous process shelf). Lateral X-ray for Meyerding grading (I-IV). Isthmic type: young gymnasts, football players. Grade III+ requires surgical consideration.

special_tests.name: Single Leg Hyperextension Test
special_tests.description: Single leg standing, extend lumbar spine. Pain at level of slippage is positive for isthmic spondylolysis.

special_tests.name: Step Deformity Palpation
special_tests.description: Palpate lumbar spinous processes. Shelf or step indicates significant spondylolisthesis.

special_tests.name: Lateral Lumbar X-ray (Flexion/Extension)
special_tests.description: Dynamic X-ray assesses instability. Meyerding grading I-IV based on slip percentage.

name: Lumbar Facet Joint Pain
description: Pain arising from the lumbar zygapophyseal joints, common in the elderly and after trauma.
key_findings: Lumbar pain worse with extension and ipsilateral rotation, localised paraspinal tenderness, referred pain to buttock/proximal thigh.
diagnostic_tips: Clinical diagnosis is unreliable — gold standard is medial branch block. Extension-rotation is most provocative. Referred pain rarely below knee (distinguishes from disc/radiculopathy). RFN effective if MBB successful.

interventions: Radiofrequency neurotomy (RFN) if MBB effective — 12 months relief

special_tests.name: Extension-Rotation Test
special_tests.description: Stand, extend and rotate lumbar spine. Ipsilateral pain indicates facet joint compression.

special_tests.name: Kemp's Test
special_tests.description: Seated extension + lateral flexion + rotation. Ipsilateral LBP indicates facet loading.

special_tests.name: Medial Branch Block
special_tests.description: Gold standard: inject local anaesthetic at medial branch. ≥80% pain relief confirms facet origin.

name: Piriformis Syndrome (Lumbar)
description: Sciatic nerve irritation by the piriformis muscle causing buttock pain and pseudoradiculopathy.
key_findings: Buttock pain, sciatic-type radiation, no lumbar signs, tender piriformis, FAIR test positive.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out lumbar disc herniation first (negative SLR, normal neurological exam). Tenderness is in the buttock (not lumbar). FAIR test stretches piriformis. Botox injection diagnostic and therapeutic.

interventions: Gluteal and hip external rotator strengthening

special_tests.name: FAIR Test
special_tests.description: Hip flexion, adduction, IR. Sciatic pain reproduction indicates piriformis compression.

special_tests.name: Pace Sign
special_tests.description: Resist abduction + ER in seated position. Pain and weakness indicate piriformis syndrome.

special_tests.name: Beatty Sign
special_tests.description: Side-lying, lift top knee. Buttock pain on affected side indicates piriformis involvement.

name: SIJ Dysfunction
description: Pain arising from the sacroiliac joint, common in pregnancy and asymmetric loading conditions.
key_findings: Posterior pelvic pain, 3 of 5 pain provocation tests positive, pain with single leg activities.
diagnostic_tips: Cluster of 3+ positive pain provocation tests is most reliable clinical diagnosis. Distinguish from lumbar facet and hip pathology. ASLR (active SLR) tests pelvic load transfer. Ankylosing spondylitis in young males with morning stiffness.

special_tests.name: FABER Test (Posterior Pain)
special_tests.description: Flex, abduct, ER hip. Posterior pelvic pain (not groin) indicates SIJ involvement.

special_tests.name: Posterior Shear (POSH) Test
special_tests.description: Posteroanterior pressure through flexed femur. Posterior pelvic pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Active Straight Leg Raise (ASLR)
special_tests.description: Supine, raise leg 5cm. Difficulty/pain improved with compression indicates SIJ/pelvic girdle dysfunction.

name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
description: Seronegative inflammatory spondyloarthropathy affecting the sacroiliac joints and spine, causing progressive stiffness.
key_findings: Insidious low back pain in young adult male (<40), morning stiffness >1 hour improving with activity, positive sacroiliac provocation tests, reduced lumbar mobility (Schober's < 5cm).
diagnostic_tips: Inflammatory back pain: insidious onset, morning stiffness >30 min, improved with exercise, night pain (awakens in second half of night), age <40. HLA-B27 and CRP/ESR. MRI shows sacroiliitis. ASAS criteria for diagnosis. Refer to rheumatology.

interventions: TNF inhibitors / IL-17 blockers if NSAID failure

special_tests.name: Modified Schober's Test
special_tests.description: Mark L5 and 10cm above. Forward flexion — <5cm increase indicates lumbar restriction in AS.

special_tests.name: Chest Expansion
special_tests.description: Thoracic excursion at 4th intercostal space. <2.5cm indicates thoracic involvement in AS.

special_tests.name: SACROILIAC Provocation Cluster
special_tests.description: 3 of 5 tests positive (Thigh Thrust, FABER, Gaenslen, Distraction, Compression) indicates SIJ involvement.

name: Osteitis Pubis
description: Stress-related bone marrow oedema and inflammation of the pubic symphysis and adjacent structures.
key_findings: Groin/pubic pain, tender pubic symphysis, pain with hip adduction and single leg loading, bilateral adductor tightness.
diagnostic_tips: Pubic symphysis palpation is diagnostic (directly and medially). Adductor squeeze test positive. MRI shows bone marrow oedema at symphysis. Distinguish from adductor strain (more lateral), inguinal hernia, sports hernia.

interventions: Gradual return to sport programme (8-12 weeks)
Corticosteroid injection into symphysis for recalcitrant cases

special_tests.name: Pubic Symphysis Palpation
special_tests.description: Direct palpation of pubic symphysis. Focal tenderness is positive.

special_tests.name: Adductor Squeeze Test
special_tests.description: Squeeze examiner fist at 0° and 45° hip flexion. Pubic/groin pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Single Leg Standing Provocation
special_tests.description: Weight-bearing on single leg. Pubic pain indicates load transfer dysfunction.

name: Pelvic Girdle Pain (PGP)
description: Pain between the posterior iliac crest and gluteal fold, including the symphysis, during pregnancy or postpartum.
key_findings: Posterior pelvic pain, positive ASLR, difficulty with single leg activities, waddling gait, pain lying in positions.
diagnostic_tips: ASLR is most useful single test. Symptomatic but normal X-ray in most (imaging not required in pregnancy). Distinguish from lumbar back pain (different location and provocation). Pelvic belt improves ASLR — confirms PGP.

interventions: Activity advice (symmetrical activities, avoid single leg loading)

special_tests.name: Active Straight Leg Raise (ASLR)
special_tests.description: Supine, raise leg 5cm. Difficulty/pain improved with compression band confirms PGP.

special_tests.name: Posterior Pelvic Pain Provocation (P4)
special_tests.description: Longitudinal force through femur with 90° hip flexion. Posterior pelvic pain is positive.

special_tests.name: Pelvic Compression Test
special_tests.description: Lateral compression over iliac crests. Reproduction of posterior pelvic pain indicates SIJ involvement.

name: Peripheral Neuropathy (Diabetic)
description: Diffuse sensorimotor peripheral nerve dysfunction in a length-dependent (stocking-glove) pattern due to chronic hyperglycaemia.
key_findings: Stocking-glove numbness/tingling, reduced vibration and proprioception, absent ankle reflexes, distal weakness in severe cases.
diagnostic_tips: Stocking-glove distinguishes from radiculopathy (dermatomal). Monofilament: 10g failure = high ulceration risk. EMG/NCS: reduced amplitudes and slowed conduction. Glycaemic control is treatment cornerstone.

special_tests.name: 10g Monofilament Test
special_tests.description: Apply 10g monofilament to plantar foot. Failure to detect = loss of protective sensation.

special_tests.name: Vibration Sense (128Hz)
special_tests.description: Apply to hallux. Absent vibration indicates large fibre neuropathy.

special_tests.name: Ankle Reflex
special_tests.description: Reduced/absent ankle reflex indicates early-moderate peripheral neuropathy.

name: Brachial Plexus Injury
description: Traction, compression or avulsion injury to the brachial plexus causing upper limb weakness and sensory loss.
key_findings: Upper trunk (C5-C6): waiter's tip posture. Lower trunk (C8-T1): Klumpke's — intrinsic hand weakness. Complete: flail arm. Horner's syndrome with T1 avulsion.
diagnostic_tips: Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) = T1 root avulsion — poor prognosis. MRI cervical + CT myelogram for avulsion. EMG/NCS essential. Pancoast: apical lung mass on CXR.

special_tests.name: Upper Limb Neurological Exam
special_tests.description: Myotomal and dermatomal assessment. Map weakness and sensory loss to plexus level.

special_tests.name: Horner's Syndrome Assessment
special_tests.description: Ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis ipsilateral. Indicates T1 root avulsion — poor prognosis.

special_tests.name: Tinel's Sign (Plexus)
special_tests.description: Tap along brachial plexus. Distal tingling suggests partial injury with regenerating axons.

name: Meralgia Paraesthetica
description: Entrapment of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) at the inguinal ligament causing anterior/lateral thigh burning pain.
key_findings: Burning, tingling or numbness in anterior/lateral thigh (LFCN distribution). No motor deficit. Worse standing/walking, relieved by sitting.
diagnostic_tips: Pure sensory nerve — no motor deficit or reflex change (distinguishes from femoral neuropathy and L2/L3 radiculopathy). Pelvic compression near ASIS reproduces symptoms.

interventions: LFCN decompression or neurectomy if failed 3 months

special_tests.name: Pelvic Compression Test
special_tests.description: Compress pelvis at ASIS for 45 sec. Lateral thigh numbness/burning reproduction is positive.

special_tests.name: LFCN Palpation (Tinel's)
special_tests.description: Palpate just medial to ASIS. Tenderness/Tinel's reproducing thigh symptoms is positive.

special_tests.name: Hip Extension Provocation
special_tests.description: Standing hip extension stretches LFCN. Reproduces anterior thigh burning.

name: Common Peroneal Nerve Palsy
description: Compression or traction injury to the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head causing foot drop and lateral leg sensory loss.
causes: Compression at fibular head (cast, leg crossing)
key_findings: Foot drop (dorsiflexion + eversion weakness), high-stepping gait, sensory loss lateral lower leg and dorsal foot, Tinel's at fibular head.
diagnostic_tips: High-stepping gait is pathognomonic. Distinguish deep peroneal (dorsiflexion only) from superficial (eversion only). EMG/NCS confirms level and prognosis. AFO for foot drop.

special_tests.name: Fibular Head Tinel's
special_tests.description: Tap over peroneal nerve at fibular neck. Tingling into dorsal foot is positive.

special_tests.name: Ankle Dorsiflexion Strength
special_tests.description: Resisted dorsiflexion. Weakness <4/5 indicates deep peroneal involvement.

special_tests.name: Foot Eversion Strength
special_tests.description: Resisted eversion. Weakness with preserved inversion = superficial peroneal involvement.

name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS)
description: Chronic neuropathic pain condition with autonomic, sensory, and motor changes disproportionate to the inciting injury.
key_findings: Burning pain disproportionate to injury, allodynia, hyperalgesia, oedema, skin colour/temperature changes, trophic changes. Type I (no nerve injury), Type II (nerve injury).
diagnostic_tips: Budapest Criteria: continuing pain disproportionate to event + signs/symptoms in ≥2 categories (sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor, motor/trophic). Early diagnosis critical. Bone scintigraphy. Distinguish from infection and DVT.

interventions: Spinal cord stimulation for refractory CRPS

special_tests.name: Budapest Criteria
special_tests.description: ≥3 of 4 symptom + ≥2 of 4 sign categories (sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor, motor/trophic). Most validated.

special_tests.name: Skin Temperature Asymmetry
special_tests.description: >1°C side-to-side difference suggests vasomotor dysfunction.

special_tests.name: Grip Strength Comparison
special_tests.description: Dynamometry side-to-side deficit correlates with CRPS severity.

name: Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome
description: Entrapment of the posterior tibial nerve beneath the flexor retinaculum on the medial ankle.
key_findings: Burning/tingling in medial ankle and plantar foot, positive Tinel's at tarsal tunnel, worse standing and walking.
diagnostic_tips: Tinel's medial to medial malleolus (posterior to tibialis posterior). Distinguish from plantar fasciitis (morning pain, calcaneal tenderness). EMG/NCS confirms. Orthoses for pronation correction.

special_tests.name: Tinel's at Tarsal Tunnel
special_tests.description: Tap posterior to medial malleolus. Paresthesia into plantar foot is positive.

special_tests.name: DF-Eversion Test
special_tests.description: Maximally dorsiflex and evert foot 5-10 sec. Plantar tingling reproduction is positive.

special_tests.name: Tibial Neural Tension
special_tests.description: Slump with ankle DF and eversion. Plantar tingling indicates tibial neural sensitivity.

name: Neuralgic Amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner)
description: Acute idiopathic brachial plexus neuritis causing severe shoulder pain followed by rapid multifocal weakness.
key_findings: Severe acute shoulder pain (burning, night pain), followed by profound patchy upper limb weakness. Serratus anterior, supraspinatus, infraspinatus most affected.
diagnostic_tips: Triphasic: severe pain → weakness as pain resolves → slow recovery. MRI plexus shows fascicular swelling. EMG/NCS maps extent. Distinguish from cervical disc (radicular pattern). 80-90% recover in 2-3 years.

special_tests.name: Brachial Plexus Mapping
special_tests.description: Patchy multifocal weakness not conforming to single root or trunk. Distinguishes from radiculopathy.

special_tests.name: MRI Brachial Plexus
special_tests.description: T2 hyperintensity and fascicular swelling. Most sensitive imaging.

special_tests.name: EMG/NCS (Multifocal)
special_tests.description: Denervation across multiple nerve territories. Confirms diagnosis.

name: Sciatic Nerve Entrapment (Deep Gluteal)
description: Entrapment of the sciatic nerve outside the spine (piriformis, hamstring origin, ischial tunnel) causing radicular-type symptoms.
key_findings: Posterior buttock and leg pain, no lumbar signs, positive FAIR test, tender deep gluteal region.
diagnostic_tips: Negative lumbar imaging + positive FAIR = non-discal. MRI pelvis (not lumbar) for deep gluteal pathology. Distinguish from disc herniation (positive SLR, Slump, lumbar signs).

interventions: Surgical deep gluteal space release if failed

special_tests.name: FAIR Test
special_tests.description: Hip flexion, adduction, IR. Sciatic pain reproduction is positive.

special_tests.name: Pace Sign
special_tests.description: Resisted hip ABD + ER seated. Pain and weakness = piriformis involvement.

special_tests.name: Negative Slump Test
special_tests.description: Negative/minimal slump with positive FAIR points to non-spinal cause.

name: Long Thoracic Nerve Palsy
description: Injury to the long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) causing serratus anterior weakness and medial scapular winging.
key_findings: Medial scapular winging (vertebral border lifts from thorax), inability to forward flex >90°, positive push-up plus test.
diagnostic_tips: Wall push-up winging = serratus (long thoracic). Distinguish from trapezius palsy (lateral winging — accessory nerve). EMG confirms. Most neuralgic amyotrophy resolves 12-24 months.

interventions: Serratus anterior facilitation as reinnervation occurs

special_tests.name: Wall Push-Up Test
special_tests.description: Push-up against wall. Medial scapular winging = serratus weakness / long thoracic nerve palsy.

special_tests.name: Forward Arm Elevation
special_tests.description: Winging accentuated above 90° = serratus involvement.

special_tests.name: EMG Serratus Anterior
special_tests.description: Fibrillation potentials and MUP changes confirm long thoracic nerve injury.

name: Fibromyalgia
description: Chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain with central sensitisation, fatigue, sleep disturbance, and cognitive dysfunction.
key_findings: Widespread pain (≥4 regions, ≥3 months), fatigue, non-restorative sleep, cognitive symptoms, multiple tender points, normal investigations.
diagnostic_tips: 2016 ACR: WPI ≥7 + SSS ≥5, or WPI 4-6 + SSS ≥9. Normal bloods, X-ray, EMG. Distinguish from inflammatory arthritis (elevated ESR/CRP), hypothyroidism (TSH), and PMR.

special_tests.name: 2016 ACR Criteria
special_tests.description: WPI ≥7 + SSS ≥5 OR WPI 4-6 + SSS ≥9. Symptoms ≥3 months.

special_tests.name: Tender Point Count
special_tests.description: 11/18 tender points (historical — superseded by ACR 2016 but still used clinically).

special_tests.name: Pressure Pain Threshold
special_tests.description: Reduced globally. Confirms widespread central sensitisation.

name: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT)
description: Thrombosis of the deep venous system of the lower limb requiring urgent anticoagulation to prevent pulmonary embolism.
key_findings: Calf swelling, warmth, erythema, tenderness, Homan's sign (low specificity). May be asymptomatic.
diagnostic_tips: RED FLAG — do NOT treat with physiotherapy until excluded. Wells DVT Score + D-dimer for risk stratification. Doppler USS gold standard. Urgent medical referral for positive findings.

interventions: Early ambulation safe — does not increase PE risk

special_tests.name: Wells DVT Score
special_tests.description: Active cancer, paralysis, immobility, local tenderness, calf swelling, pitting oedema, collateral veins, prior DVT. Score ≥2 = high risk.

special_tests.name: Compression Duplex USS
special_tests.description: Non-compressible vein segment is diagnostic. Gold standard.

special_tests.name: D-Dimer
special_tests.description: Low D-dimer + low Wells = DVT excluded. High D-dimer → proceed to USS.

name: Chronic Exertional Compartment Syndrome
description: Reversible exercise-induced ischaemia within an osteofascial compartment causing reproducible exertional leg pain.
key_findings: Reproducible leg pain at consistent distance/time, resolves within 30 min rest, neurological symptoms in severe cases, skin tightness during exertion.
diagnostic_tips: Bilateral in 75%. Intracompartmental pressure measurement is gold standard. Rule out stress fracture and PAES. Gait retraining may reduce tibial stress before surgery.

special_tests.name: Intracompartmental Pressure
special_tests.description: Post-exercise >30mmHg at 1 min is positive. Gold standard.

special_tests.name: Exertional Provocation
special_tests.description: Reproduce symptoms running at threshold speed. Assess onset and resolution time.

special_tests.name: Post-Exercise Neurological Assessment
special_tests.description: Transient sensory/motor deficit post-exercise indicates nerve ischaemia.

name: Popliteal Artery Entrapment Syndrome
description: Extrinsic compression of the popliteal artery by anomalous gastrocnemius causing exercise-induced claudication in young athletes.
key_findings: Exercise-induced posterior calf claudication in young athlete, resolves with rest, pulse diminution on resisted plantarflexion.
diagnostic_tips: Young athlete + claudication = always consider PAES. ABI drops with provocation. Duplex USS during provocative manoeuvres. MRI/CT angiogram confirms. Distinguish from CECS (bilateral, no pulse change).

special_tests.name: Provocative Pulse Test
special_tests.description: Pedal pulses with passive DF and active PF against resistance. Pulse obliteration is positive.

special_tests.name: ABI with Provocation
special_tests.description: ABI at rest vs post-exercise. Drop >0.3 indicates popliteal compromise.

special_tests.name: Dynamic Duplex USS
special_tests.description: Artery compression visible during provocative plantarflexion in real time.

name: Spondyloarthropathy (PsA / ReA)
description: Seronegative inflammatory arthropathy affecting spine, SIJ, and peripheral joints — including psoriatic arthritis and reactive arthritis.
causes: Reactive arthritis: Chlamydia, Salmonella, Campylobacter trigger
key_findings: PsA: asymmetric oligoarthritis, dactylitis, enthesitis, psoriasis. ReA: arthritis + urethritis + conjunctivitis (Reiter's triad). Both: sacroiliitis, inflammatory back pain.
diagnostic_tips: PsA: check skin and nails (pitting, onycholysis). ReA: recent GI/GU infection. CASPAR criteria for PsA. X-ray: 'pencil in cup' in PsA. Refer rheumatology for biologics.

interventions: Enthesitis: eccentric loading after inflammation controlled

special_tests.name: CASPAR Criteria (PsA)
special_tests.description: Score ≥3: psoriasis, nail lesions, RF negative, dactylitis, juxta-articular new bone.

special_tests.name: Dactylitis Assessment
special_tests.description: Diffuse digit swelling (sausage finger/toe). Pathognomonic for PsA/ReA.

special_tests.name: Enthesitis Score (LEI)
special_tests.description: Leeds Enthesitis Index — palpation of Achilles, plantar fascia, patellar, and epicondyle insertions.

name: Median Nerve Neuropathy (AIN / Pronator)
description: Median nerve injury at a level other than the carpal tunnel — AIN palsy or pronator syndrome.
causes: Pronator syndrome: fibrous band, pronator hypertrophy
High median nerve: forearm lacerations or fractures
key_findings: AIN: inability to make OK sign (FPL + FDP index weakness), no sensory deficit. Pronator: median paresthesia with resisted pronation, no nocturnal symptoms.
diagnostic_tips: OK sign failure = AIN palsy. No sensory loss distinguishes AIN from CTS. Resisted pronation provocative for pronator syndrome. EMG distinguishes level.

interventions: Monitor FPL and FDP index strength
Surgical decompression/neurolysis if no recovery at 6 months

special_tests.name: OK Sign Test
special_tests.description: Ask patient to make OK circle. Inability indicates AIN palsy (FPL + FDP index).

special_tests.name: Resisted Pronation Tinel's
special_tests.description: Resist pronation pressing over pronator teres. Median paresthesia = pronator syndrome.

special_tests.name: Pinch Strength
special_tests.description: Key pinch dynamometry. Pulp-to-pulp weakness indicates AIN or anterior median nerve.

name: Intercostal Neuralgia
description: Neuropathic pain along an intercostal nerve distribution from irritation, compression, or damage — commonly post-herpetic.
key_findings: Dermatomal band of burning/shooting chest pain, allodynia/hyperalgesia, worse deep breathing and coughing.
diagnostic_tips: Post-herpetic: precede with zoster rash. Confirm dermatomal distribution. Exclude cardiac (ECG/troponin first). Thoracic X-ray for fracture. Always exclude malignancy if no clear cause.

interventions: Antivirals (acyclovir) within 72 hours for active zoster

special_tests.name: Intercostal Dermatomal Mapping
special_tests.description: Map sensory changes (allodynia, hyperalgesia) to intercostal dermatome levels.

special_tests.name: Rib Compression Test
special_tests.description: Lateral thoracic compression. Dermatomal pain provocation indicates costovertebral origin.

special_tests.name: Thoracic Neural Tension
special_tests.description: Slump with thoracic focus. Dermatomal symptoms = neural component.

name: Cervical Facet Joint Syndrome
description: Pain originating from cervical zygapophyseal joints, common at C2-3 and C5-6.
key_findings: Unilateral neck pain, may refer to head/shoulder. Pain with extension and ipsilateral rotation. Tender over facet joints. No neurological deficit.
diagnostic_tips: No single reliable clinical test. Pain pattern helps: C2-3 refers to occiput, C5-6 to supraspinous fossa. Diagnostic block is gold standard. Rule out disc and nerve root pathology.

treatment_plan.acute: Manual therapy (Maitland mobilisation), heat, gentle ROM
treatment_plan.subacute: Cervical stabilisation exercises, postural correction, manual therapy
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive exercise programme, consider facet joint injection if refractory

differential_diagnosis: Cervical disc pathology, myofascial pain, cervical radiculopathy, cervicogenic headache

name: SLAP Lesion (Superior Labral Tear)
description: Tear of the superior glenoid labrum from anterior to posterior, involving biceps anchor.
key_findings: Deep shoulder pain with overhead activities. Painful clicking/catching. Pain with biceps-loaded activities. Positive O'Brien's test, Speed's test.
diagnostic_tips: SLAP lesions are difficult to diagnose clinically. Combination of tests improves accuracy. MR arthrography is gold standard. Type II most clinically significant. Age >40 consider conservative first.

treatment_plan.acute: Activity modification, anti-inflammatory, gentle ROM
treatment_plan.subacute: Rotator cuff and scapular strengthening, avoid provocative positions
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive overhead strengthening, consider surgical repair if failed conservative (especially Type II in young athletes)

differential_diagnosis: Rotator cuff tear, biceps tendinopathy, instability, AC joint pathology

name: Lateral Epicondylalgia (Tennis Elbow)
description: Tendinopathy of the common extensor origin, primarily ECRB, characterised by degenerative changes.
key_findings: Lateral elbow pain with gripping, wrist extension. Tender over lateral epicondyle. Pain with resisted wrist extension and middle finger extension. Grip strength reduced.
diagnostic_tips: Not inflammatory - it's a tendinopathy (degenerative). Cozen's test and Mill's test. Check for radial nerve involvement (radial tunnel). Pain-free grip strength is reliable outcome measure.

treatment_plan.acute: Load modification, isometric wrist extension, counterforce brace
treatment_plan.subacute: Eccentric wrist extension programme, Tyler twist (FlexBar)
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy slow resistance, address kinetic chain, consider PRP if refractory

differential_diagnosis: Radial tunnel syndrome, posterior interosseous nerve entrapment, cervical radiculopathy C6, radiohumeral joint pathology

name: Medial Epicondylalgia (Golfer's Elbow)
description: Tendinopathy of the common flexor-pronator origin at medial epicondyle.
key_findings: Medial elbow pain with gripping and wrist flexion. Tender over medial epicondyle. Pain with resisted wrist flexion and pronation.
diagnostic_tips: Less common than lateral epicondylalgia (3-10x). Check ulnar nerve (50% have concomitant ulnar neuropathy). Reverse Tyler Twist for treatment.

treatment_plan.acute: Load modification, isometric wrist flexion, bracing
treatment_plan.subacute: Eccentric wrist flexion, reverse Tyler twist
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy slow resistance, address throwing mechanics if applicable

differential_diagnosis: Ulnar nerve entrapment, MCL sprain, cervical radiculopathy C7-T1, medial epicondyle fracture

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament (UCL) Sprain
description: Injury to the medial ulnar collateral ligament of the elbow, common in overhead throwing athletes.
key_findings: Medial elbow pain with throwing. Tenderness over UCL. Positive valgus stress test at 30° flexion. May have ulnar nerve symptoms.
diagnostic_tips: Moving valgus stress test is most specific. Pain during late cocking/early acceleration phase of throw. MR arthrography is gold standard imaging. Milking maneuver also useful.

treatment_plan.acute: Rest from throwing, ice, NSAIDs, brace
treatment_plan.subacute: Forearm and wrist strengthening, gradual ROM
treatment_plan.chronic: Throwing programme progression. Tommy John surgery (UCL reconstruction) if failed conservative in throwing athletes.

differential_diagnosis: Medial epicondylalgia, ulnar nerve entrapment, medial epicondyle apophysitis, flexor-pronator strain

name: TFCC Tear (Triangular Fibrocartilage Complex)
description: Injury to the triangular fibrocartilage complex, the primary stabiliser of the DRUJ.
key_findings: Ulnar-sided wrist pain. Pain with forearm rotation under load. Positive press test (push up from chair). Clicking with rotation.
diagnostic_tips: Fovea sign (tenderness in soft spot between FCU and ulnar styloid) is most reliable. Press test is useful functional test. MR arthrography is gold standard imaging.

treatment_plan.acute: Immobilisation, wrist brace, avoid rotational loading
treatment_plan.subacute: Graduated forearm rotation exercises, grip strengthening
treatment_plan.chronic: Arthroscopic repair or debridement if failed conservative

differential_diagnosis: ECU tendinopathy, DRUJ instability, ulnar impaction syndrome, pisotriquetral OA

name: Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)
description: Morphological abnormality of femoral head (cam), acetabulum (pincer), or both causing impingement.
causes: Cam morphology (bony bump on femoral head-neck junction)
key_findings: Anterior hip/groin pain with flexion activities. Pain sitting for prolonged periods. Positive FADIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation). Reduced hip IR in flexion. C-sign.
diagnostic_tips: FADIR is sensitive but not specific. AP pelvis and Dunn view X-rays for morphology. Alpha angle >55° suggests cam. Lateral CE angle >40° suggests pincer. MRA for labral tear assessment.

treatment_plan.acute: Activity modification, avoid deep flexion, NSAIDs
treatment_plan.subacute: Hip strengthening (especially glute med/max), core stability, ROM within pain-free range
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive hip strengthening, movement retraining. Arthroscopic surgery if failed 3-6 months conservative.

differential_diagnosis: Hip labral tear, hip OA, adductor strain, sports hernia, stress fracture

name: Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome
description: Lateral hip pain encompassing gluteal tendinopathy, trochanteric bursitis, and ITB friction.
key_findings: Lateral hip pain worse with side-lying, stair climbing, single leg stance. Tender over greater trochanter. Positive single leg stand test (30 seconds). Trendelenburg positive in some.
diagnostic_tips: Term 'trochanteric bursitis' is outdated - gluteal tendinopathy is primary pathology. Single leg stand for 30 sec is most useful clinical test. Avoid adduction-based stretches (compresses tendon). Load management is key.

treatment_plan.acute: Avoid crossing legs, side-lying on affected side. Isometric hip abduction
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive hip abduction strengthening avoiding adduction stretches
treatment_plan.chronic: Functional strengthening, running gait retraining if applicable. Shockwave if refractory.

differential_diagnosis: Hip OA, lumbar referred pain, meralgia paraesthetica, stress fracture, SIJ dysfunction

name: Meniscal Tear
description: Tear of the medial or lateral meniscus, acting as shock absorbers in the knee.
key_findings: Joint line tenderness. Locking or catching. Effusion. Positive McMurray's test. Pain with deep squat. Medial more common than lateral.
diagnostic_tips: McMurray's is specific but not sensitive. Thessaly test at 20° flexion is most accurate overall. Joint line tenderness is sensitive but not specific. MRI for confirmation. Degenerative tears common in >40y - may not need surgery.

treatment_plan.acute: RICE, protected weight bearing, gentle ROM
treatment_plan.subacute: Quadriceps and hamstring strengthening, neuromuscular training
treatment_plan.chronic: Most degenerative tears respond to exercise therapy. Surgical meniscectomy if mechanical symptoms persist. Meniscal repair for peripheral tears in young patients.

differential_diagnosis: ACL/PCL tear, OA, patellar pathology, pes anserinus bursitis, loose body

name: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome
description: Anterior knee pain arising from the patellofemoral joint, most common knee complaint in active populations.
key_findings: Anterior knee pain with stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting (movie sign). Patellar compression pain. Positive Clarke's test. Weak hip abductors.
diagnostic_tips: Hip strengthening is as important as knee strengthening. Address the entire kinetic chain. Clarke's test has poor diagnostic accuracy. Use pain with squat and step-down as functional tests. Imaging rarely needed.

treatment_plan.acute: Load modification, patellar taping, ice
treatment_plan.subacute: Quad and hip strengthening, patellar taping, orthotics if indicated
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive loading, running gait retraining, education on load management

differential_diagnosis: Patellar tendinopathy, plica syndrome, fat pad irritation, meniscal tear, osteochondral defect

name: Lateral Ankle Sprain
description: Injury to the lateral ankle ligament complex, most commonly ATFL, then CFL, then PTFL.
key_findings: Lateral ankle swelling and ecchymosis. Tender over ATFL (anterior to lateral malleolus). Positive anterior drawer (ATFL). Positive talar tilt (CFL). Grade I-III classification.
diagnostic_tips: Ottawa Ankle Rules to exclude fracture. Anterior drawer tests ATFL. Talar tilt tests CFL. Previous sprain is #1 risk factor for future sprain. Functional rehabilitation is superior to immobilisation.

treatment_plan.acute: PRICE, early weight bearing as tolerated, ankle brace
treatment_plan.subacute: Balance training, peroneal strengthening, ankle ROM
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive sport-specific training, proprioception, consider brace for sport. Chronic instability: surgical reconstruction if failed conservative.

differential_diagnosis: Ankle fracture, peroneal tendon injury, osteochondral lesion, syndesmosis sprain (high ankle), subtalar sprain

name: Plantar Fasciitis (Plantar Heel Pain)
description: Degenerative condition of the plantar fascia at its calcaneal insertion causing heel pain.
key_findings: First-step pain in morning or after rest. Plantar medial heel tenderness. Pain with prolonged standing/walking. Windlass test positive. Often bilateral.
diagnostic_tips: Windlass test is most useful clinical test. Heel spur on X-ray is incidental (not causative). Address calf tightness (key modifiable factor). High-load strength training is effective (Rathleff 2015).

treatment_plan.acute: Plantar fascia stretching, calf stretching, arch taping, night splint
treatment_plan.subacute: High-load strength training (heavy slow heel raises with towel under toes), intrinsic foot strengthening
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive loading, orthotics if biomechanical need, ESWT if refractory. Surgery last resort.

differential_diagnosis: Fat pad atrophy, calcaneal stress fracture, tarsal tunnel syndrome, Baxter's nerve entrapment, plantar fascia rupture

name: MCL Sprain (Knee)
description: Medial collateral ligament injury from valgus stress, classified Grade I-III.
key_findings: Medial knee pain and swelling. Tender over MCL. Positive valgus stress test at 30° flexion. Grade III: complete tear, instability.
diagnostic_tips: Valgus stress at 30° tests MCL. At 0° extension tests both MCL and posterior capsule/cruciate. Most MCL sprains heal conservatively (good blood supply). Check for concurrent ACL/meniscal injury.

treatment_plan.acute: Hinged knee brace, protected weight bearing, ice
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive ROM and strengthening, remove brace 4-6 weeks
treatment_plan.chronic: Full strengthening and return to sport. Surgery rarely needed except Grade III with concurrent injuries.

differential_diagnosis: ACL tear, meniscal tear, pes anserinus bursitis, medial plica, tibial plateau fracture

name: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
description: Compression of neurovascular structures in the thoracic outlet between scalenes, first rib, and clavicle.
key_findings: Arm pain, numbness, tingling (neurogenic - most common). May have vascular symptoms (colour changes, swelling). Positive Roos test, ULTT, Adson's. T1 dermatomal symptoms.
diagnostic_tips: Neurogenic TOS is most common (95%). Roos test (elevated arm stress test 3 min) is most reliable. Adson's has poor specificity. Differentiate from cervical radiculopathy and CTS. NCS/EMG helps.

treatment_plan.acute: Postural correction, scalene stretching, avoid provocative positions
treatment_plan.subacute: Shoulder girdle strengthening, neural mobilisation, first rib mobilisation
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive postural and strengthening programme. Surgery (first rib resection/scalenectomy) for refractory cases.

differential_diagnosis: Cervical radiculopathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, ulnar neuropathy, Pancoast tumour, brachial plexus injury

name: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome
description: Compression of the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel (medial elbow), second most common upper extremity neuropathy.
key_findings: Numbness in ring and little finger. Weakness of intrinsic hand muscles. Positive elbow flexion test. Positive Tinel's at cubital tunnel. Froment's sign in advanced cases.
diagnostic_tips: Elbow flexion test (hold full flexion 60 sec) is most sensitive clinical test. Tinel's less reliable. Froment's sign indicates advanced disease (adductor pollicis weakness). NCS/EMG for severity grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Avoid sustained elbow flexion, night splint in extension, nerve gliding
treatment_plan.subacute: Ulnar nerve gliding exercises, elbow pad, ergonomic modification
treatment_plan.chronic: Transposition surgery if failed conservative 3 months or progressive weakness.

differential_diagnosis: Cervical radiculopathy C8-T1, Guyon's canal syndrome, medial epicondylalgia, thoracic outlet syndrome

name: Hamstring Strain (Grade I-III)
description: Muscle strain of the hamstring group, most commonly biceps femoris at the musculotendinous junction.
key_findings: Sudden posterior thigh pain during sprinting/stretching. Ecchymosis tracking distally. Tender at injury site. Reduced knee flexion strength. Grade I: mild, II: moderate with weakness, III: complete rupture.
diagnostic_tips: Biceps femoris most common in sprinting. Semimembranosus more common in dancers/kickers. MRI within 24-48h for grading. Proximal tendon avulsion = surgical consideration. Nordic hamstring curls for prevention.

treatment_plan.acute: PRICE, protected ambulation, gentle isometrics when pain allows
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive strengthening: isometric → concentric → eccentric → functional
treatment_plan.chronic: Nordic hamstring programme, running progression, sport-specific drills. Return to sport criteria: pain-free, >90% strength symmetry.

differential_diagnosis: Sciatic nerve referred pain, hamstring tendinopathy, posterior thigh compartment syndrome, adductor strain, ischial stress fracture

name: Quadriceps Strain
description: Strain of the quadriceps muscle group, most commonly rectus femoris.
key_findings: Anterior thigh pain with resisted knee extension and hip flexion. Tender at injury site. Bruising may appear. Reduced quadriceps strength. May have palpable defect in severe cases.
diagnostic_tips: Rectus femoris is most commonly strained (crosses two joints). Grade with MRI. Myositis ossificans risk after severe contusion - avoid aggressive stretching early.

treatment_plan.acute: PRICE, gentle ROM, crutches if needed
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive quad strengthening, stretching when comfortable
treatment_plan.chronic: Full strengthening, return to sport with >90% symmetry

differential_diagnosis: Femoral stress fracture, meralgia paraesthetica, hip flexor strain, myositis ossificans, referred lumbar pain

name: Calf Strain (Gastrocnemius/Soleus)
description: Muscle strain of the gastrocnemius or soleus, often called 'tennis leg' for gastrocnemius.
key_findings: Sudden calf pain during push-off activity. Tennis leg = medial gastroc head at MTJ. Positive Thompson test rules out Achilles rupture. Bruising tracks distally.
diagnostic_tips: Must rule out Achilles tendon rupture (Thompson test, palpable gap). Must rule out DVT (Homan's sign, Wells score). Ultrasound for grading. Gastroc injured with knee extended, soleus with knee flexed.

treatment_plan.acute: PRICE, heel raise in shoe, gentle ankle ROM
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive calf loading: isometric → bilateral → single leg
treatment_plan.chronic: Eccentric calf programme, return to running progression

differential_diagnosis: Achilles tendon rupture, DVT, plantaris rupture, popliteal cyst rupture, compartment syndrome

name: ITB Syndrome (Runner's Knee)
description: Lateral knee pain from ITB friction/compression over the lateral femoral epicondyle.
key_findings: Lateral knee pain at lateral femoral epicondyle. Worse with running, especially downhill. Painful at ~30° knee flexion (impingement zone). Positive Noble's compression test.
diagnostic_tips: Noble compression test at 30° flexion is most reliable. Ober's test for ITB tightness (but ITB is not actually 'tight' - it's a compression issue). Address hip weakness, not ITB stretching. Load management is key.

treatment_plan.acute: Load modification, avoid provocative activities, ice
treatment_plan.subacute: Hip abductor strengthening, running gait retraining (increase cadence)
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive running programme, strength maintenance. Corticosteroid injection if refractory.

differential_diagnosis: Lateral meniscal tear, LCL sprain, popliteus tendinopathy, lateral compartment OA, proximal tibiofibular joint dysfunction

name: Thoracolumbar Fascia Dysfunction
description: Dysfunction of the thoracolumbar fascia contributing to low back pain and movement dysfunction.
key_findings: Widespread low back stiffness. Reduced spinal mobility. Tender thoracolumbar region. May have altered skin rolling mobility. Often associated with chronic LBP.
diagnostic_tips: Emerging area of research. Skin rolling test may identify fascial restriction. Ultrasound can show fascial thickening. Often coexists with other spinal pathologies. Manual therapy to fascia can be beneficial.

treatment_plan.acute: Foam rolling, gentle mobility exercises, manual fascial techniques
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive spinal mobility and strengthening, fascial manipulation
treatment_plan.chronic: Comprehensive exercise programme addressing muscle and fascial system

differential_diagnosis: Lumbar disc pathology, facet joint syndrome, myofascial pain syndrome, SIJ dysfunction, ankylosing spondylitis

name: Osgood-Schlatter Disease
description: Traction apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity in adolescents during growth spurts.
key_findings: Anterior knee pain at tibial tuberosity in adolescent (10-15y). Tender, swollen tibial tuberosity. Pain with running, jumping, kneeling. Prominent tibial tuberosity.
diagnostic_tips: Clinical diagnosis in appropriate age group. X-ray may show fragmentation of tibial tuberosity. Bilateral in 20-30%. Self-limiting condition resolving with skeletal maturity. Do not confuse with tibial tuberosity fracture.

treatment_plan.acute: Activity modification (not complete rest), ice, quad stretching
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive quad strengthening, hamstring flexibility
treatment_plan.chronic: Self-limiting with growth plate closure. Rarely persistent bump after maturity.

differential_diagnosis: Patellar tendinopathy, Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease, tibial tuberosity fracture, osteochondroma

name: Ankle Syndesmosis Sprain (High Ankle Sprain)
description: Injury to the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis, disrupting the interosseous membrane and ligaments.
key_findings: Anterior ankle pain above joint line. Tender over syndesmosis. Positive squeeze test. Positive external rotation stress test. Slower recovery than lateral ankle sprain.
diagnostic_tips: Squeeze test is most reliable (compress fibula to tibia at mid-calf = pain at syndesmosis). External rotation stress test at neutral dorsiflexion. Weight-bearing CT is most accurate imaging. Takes 2-3x longer to recover than lateral sprain.

treatment_plan.acute: Protected weight bearing, CAM boot, avoid dorsiflexion
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive weight bearing, gentle ROM avoiding rotation
treatment_plan.chronic: Prolonged rehab (6-12 weeks). Surgical fixation if diastasis on weight-bearing imaging.

differential_diagnosis: Lateral ankle sprain, distal fibula fracture, osteochondral lesion, peroneal tendon injury, deltoid ligament sprain

name: Posterior Ankle Impingement
description: Compression of structures at the posterior ankle, often involving os trigonum, in plantarflexion.
key_findings: Posterior ankle pain with forced plantarflexion. Pain with en pointe (ballet) or kicking. Tender posterior to lateral malleolus. Positive posterior impingement test.
diagnostic_tips: Posterior impingement test (forced plantarflexion) reproduces pain. Os trigonum visible on lateral X-ray. Must differentiate from Achilles pathology and FHL tendinopathy. Local anaesthetic injection diagnostic.

treatment_plan.acute: Avoid end-range plantarflexion, taping, anti-inflammatory
treatment_plan.subacute: Ankle ROM (avoid impingement), strengthening, biomechanical correction
treatment_plan.chronic: Arthroscopic excision of os trigonum if failed conservative

differential_diagnosis: Achilles tendinopathy, FHL tendinopathy, retrocalcaneal bursitis, subtalar pathology, calcaneal stress fracture

name: Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction
description: Pain arising from the sacroiliac joint, representing 15-25% of mechanical low back pain.
key_findings: Unilateral buttock pain below L5. Positive cluster: distraction, compression, thigh thrust, sacral thrust, Gaenslen's. At least 3 of 5 provocation tests positive is diagnostic.
diagnostic_tips: No single test is reliable - use provocation test cluster (3/5 positive = 94% specificity). Pain pointing test (Fortin finger sign) is useful screening. Diagnostic injection is gold standard. Screen young males for ankylosing spondylitis.

treatment_plan.acute: SIJ belt, manual therapy (MET, mobilisation), pelvic floor activation
treatment_plan.subacute: Lumbopelvic stabilisation, address biomechanics, motor control
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive exercise programme, pelvic belt for activity. Prolotherapy or injection if refractory.

differential_diagnosis: Lumbar disc herniation, facet joint syndrome, hip pathology, piriformis syndrome, ankylosing spondylitis

name: Subacromial Bursitis
description: Inflammation of the subacromial bursa causing shoulder pain with overhead activities.
key_findings: Painful arc 60-120°. Night pain. Positive Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer tests. Pain with overhead activities.
diagnostic_tips: Often coexists with rotator cuff tendinopathy. Cannot reliably separate from rotator cuff pathology clinically. Ultrasound shows thickened bursa. Subacromial injection test can be diagnostic.

treatment_plan.acute: Ice, activity modification, subacromial injection if severe
treatment_plan.subacute: Rotator cuff and scapular strengthening
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive strengthening programme

differential_diagnosis: Rotator cuff tendinopathy, rotator cuff tear, calcific tendinitis, AC joint pathology

name: Biceps Tendinopathy
description: Tendinopathy of the long head of biceps tendon in the bicipital groove.
key_findings: Anterior shoulder pain in bicipital groove. Positive Speed's test. Positive Yergason's test. Pain with lifting and overhead activities.
diagnostic_tips: Rarely occurs in isolation - usually secondary to rotator cuff or labral pathology. Speed's and Yergason's have poor diagnostic accuracy in isolation. Ultrasound can assess tendon integrity.

treatment_plan.acute: Activity modification, ice, gentle pendulum exercises
treatment_plan.subacute: Rotator cuff and scapular strengthening, avoid painful positions
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive strengthening. Consider tenotomy/tenodesis if refractory.

differential_diagnosis: Rotator cuff pathology, SLAP lesion, AC joint pathology, cervical radiculopathy

name: Calcific Tendinitis (Shoulder)
description: Calcium hydroxyapatite deposition within the rotator cuff tendons, most commonly supraspinatus.
key_findings: Acute severe shoulder pain (resorptive phase). Extremely painful and restricted ROM. X-ray shows calcification in supraspinatus tendon. May present as acute or chronic.
diagnostic_tips: X-ray diagnostic. Acute phase (resorptive) is most painful but has best prognosis. Chronic phase (formative) is less painful but may cause impingement. Ultrasound-guided needling (barbotage) effective for chronic.

treatment_plan.acute: NSAIDS, subacromial corticosteroid injection, gentle pendulum exercises
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive ROM and rotator cuff strengthening as pain settles
treatment_plan.chronic: ESWT or ultrasound-guided barbotage for persistent calcifications. Arthroscopic removal if refractory.

differential_diagnosis: Rotator cuff tear, subacromial impingement, adhesive capsulitis, glenohumeral OA

name: Cervical Disc Degeneration
description: Age-related degenerative changes of the cervical intervertebral discs.
key_findings: Neck pain and stiffness. Reduced ROM. May have referred pain to shoulder/scapula. No neurological deficit (unless associated stenosis/HNP).
diagnostic_tips: Very common on imaging in asymptomatic individuals (>50% by age 40). MRI findings must correlate with clinical presentation. Do not over-diagnose. Conservative management effective for most.

treatment_plan.acute: Education, gentle ROM, manual therapy
treatment_plan.subacute: Cervical stabilisation, postural correction
treatment_plan.chronic: Ongoing exercise programme, ergonomic optimisation

differential_diagnosis: Cervical radiculopathy, cervical myelopathy, myofascial pain, facet joint syndrome

name: Ankle Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the talocrural joint.
key_findings: Ankle pain with weight bearing. Stiffness. Reduced dorsiflexion. Osteophyte formation (anterior impingement). Antalgic gait.
diagnostic_tips: Unlike hip/knee OA, ankle OA is primarily post-traumatic. Weight-bearing X-rays for grading. Anterior impingement osteophytes are common. Manual therapy + exercise effective for mild-moderate.

treatment_plan.acute: Activity modification, orthotics, bracing
treatment_plan.subacute: Ankle ROM exercises, strengthening, manual therapy
treatment_plan.chronic: Progressive exercise, rocker-bottom shoe. Ankle replacement or fusion for severe refractory cases.

differential_diagnosis: Ankle impingement, OCD, tarsal coalition, inflammatory arthritis, subtalar OA

name: Trigger Finger (Stenosing Tenosynovitis)
description: Stenosing tenosynovitis of the finger flexor tendons at the A1 pulley causing catching/locking.
key_findings: Clicking/catching with finger flexion/extension. May lock in flexion. Tender nodule at A1 pulley (MCP joint level). Thumb, ring, and middle fingers most common.
diagnostic_tips: Clinical diagnosis is straightforward. Grade: I (pain/tenderness), II (catching), III (locking, passively correctable), IV (locked). Corticosteroid injection is highly effective (first-line). Multiple finger involvement → screen for diabetes.

treatment_plan.acute: Splinting MCP in extension, activity modification, NSAID
treatment_plan.subacute: Corticosteroid injection at A1 pulley (80% success rate)
treatment_plan.chronic: Surgical A1 pulley release if failed 2 injections. Percutaneous release is alternative.

differential_diagnosis: Dupuytren's contracture, flexor tendon rupture, MCP joint pathology, ganglion cyst

name: Costochondritis
description: Inflammation of the costochondral or costosternal joints causing anterior chest pain.
key_findings: Anterior chest wall pain, reproducible with palpation. Tender over costochondral junctions. Pain with deep breathing, coughing, trunk rotation. Usually self-limiting.
diagnostic_tips: CRITICAL: Must rule out cardiac causes first in acute presentations. Reproducibility with palpation helps differentiate from cardiac pain but does not rule it out. ECG if any doubt. Self-limiting in most cases (weeks to months).

treatment_plan.acute: NSAIDs, ice, activity modification, reassurance
treatment_plan.subacute: Gentle stretching of pectorals, postural correction
treatment_plan.chronic: Usually self-limiting. Manual therapy to costovertebral joints if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome, PE, pneumothorax, Tietze's syndrome, rib fracture, intercostal neuralgia

name: Erector Spinae Strain
description: Muscle strain of the erector spinae group due to overload, poor mechanics, or sudden forceful movement.
key_findings: Localised paraspinal tenderness, pain with spinal extension and lateral flexion, muscle guarding, reduced lumbar ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Palpate paraspinal muscles for spasm and tenderness. Pain typically unilateral. Distinguish from disc pathology by absence of radiculopathy. Check for facet joint involvement.

interventions: Bird dog and prone cobra exercises

name: Thoracic Spine Dysfunction
description: Thoracic spine hypomobility or dysfunction leading to compensatory movement in cervical/lumbar spine and shoulders.
key_findings: Reduced thoracic rotation and extension, increased kyphosis, compensatory cervical/lumbar hypermobility, rib cage stiffness.
diagnostic_tips: Assess segmental mobility with PA pressures. Thoracic rotation should be ~35° each side. Check rib mobility. Often contributes to shoulder impingement and neck pain.

interventions: Thoracic extension mobilisation over foam roller
Prone cobra and thoracic extension exercises

name: Spondylolisthesis
description: Forward slippage of one vertebral body on the one below, commonly at L4-L5 or L5-S1.
key_findings: Palpable step deformity, pain with extension, hamstring tightness, possible radiculopathy if nerve root compressed.
diagnostic_tips: Standing lateral X-ray is diagnostic. Grade severity (Meyerding I-V). Stork test for pars fracture. Distinguish isthmic from degenerative type.

interventions: Core stabilisation (especially transversus abdominis and multifidus)
Sport-specific return with bracing if needed

name: Quadratus Lumborum Syndrome
description: Pain and dysfunction arising from quadratus lumborum strain, trigger points, or spasm, often mimicking SI joint or disc pathology.
causes: Sudden twisting or lateral bending under load
key_findings: Lateral low back pain, pain with side bending, tenderness between 12th rib and iliac crest. Pain may refer to SI joint, hip, or groin. Positive side-lying QL stretch reproduction.
diagnostic_tips: QL trigger points refer to SI joint and lateral hip — distinguish from true SI dysfunction. Palpate between 12th rib and iliac crest posterolaterally. Check for leg length discrepancy. Often missed as 'non-specific LBP'.

interventions: Trigger point dry needling or manual release
Suitcase carry and anti-lateral flexion exercises
Correction of leg length discrepancy if present
Hip strengthening (glute med) to reduce QL compensation

differential_diagnosis: SI joint dysfunction, lumbar disc herniation, kidney pathology (renal colic), hip pathology, lumbar facet syndrome

name: Pectoralis Minor Syndrome (PMI)
description: Compression of neurovascular structures beneath a tight or shortened pectoralis minor, contributing to thoracic outlet symptoms and scapular dyskinesis.
key_findings: Anterior chest tightness, rounded shoulders, limited pec minor length (<7.6cm from coracoid to 4th rib), possible thoracic outlet symptoms (numbness in hand).
diagnostic_tips: Measure pec minor length: coracoid to 4th rib. Wright's test for neurovascular compression. Distinguish from thoracic outlet syndrome, cervical radiculopathy. Often co-exists with scapular dyskinesis.

interventions: Progressive serratus anterior and lower trapezius strengthening
Return to overhead activity with corrected mechanics

differential_diagnosis: Thoracic outlet syndrome, cervical radiculopathy (C8-T1), cardiac referred pain, costochondritis

name: Latissimus Dorsi Strain
description: Strain or tear of the latissimus dorsi muscle, common in overhead athletes (baseball, swimming, climbing) and heavy lifting.
key_findings: Posterior axillary fold pain, pain with resisted shoulder extension/adduction/IR, tenderness along lateral trunk. Possible palpable defect in severe tears.
diagnostic_tips: Resisted shoulder extension and IR reproduce pain. Palpate posterior axillary fold and lateral trunk. MRI for grading tears. Distinguish from teres major strain and triceps long head pathology.

interventions: Activity modification — avoid overhead and pulling
Gentle lat stretch within pain tolerance
Progressive lat strengthening (straight arm pulldown, rows)

differential_diagnosis: Teres major strain, long head of triceps injury, posterior shoulder instability, thoracolumbar fascia strain

name: Pes Anserinus Tendinopathy/Bursitis
description: Inflammation of the pes anserinus bursa or tendons (sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus) at the medial proximal tibia.
key_findings: Medial knee pain 5-7cm below joint line, tenderness over pes anserinus insertion, pain worse with stairs and getting up from sitting. Swelling may be present.
diagnostic_tips: Tenderness is BELOW the joint line — distinguish from MCL sprain (at joint line) and medial meniscus (at joint line with McMurray's). Common in obese females with OA knee. Check for associated conditions.

interventions: Corticosteroid injection to bursa if severe

special_tests.name: Valgus Stress Test (to rule out MCL)

differential_diagnosis: MCL sprain, medial meniscus tear, medial compartment OA, tibial stress fracture

name: Supraspinatus Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Supraspinatus tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Supraspinatus resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Supraspinatus with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Supraspinatus, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Supraspinatus, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Supraspinatus during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Supraspinatus, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Supraspinatus characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Supraspinatus joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Supraspinatus-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Supraspinatus muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Supraspinatus bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Supraspinatus Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Supraspinatus due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Infraspinatus tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Strain
