description: Acute muscular injury of the Infraspinatus resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Infraspinatus with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Infraspinatus, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Infraspinatus, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Infraspinatus during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Infraspinatus, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Infraspinatus characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Infraspinatus joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Infraspinatus-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Infraspinatus muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Infraspinatus bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Infraspinatus Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Infraspinatus due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Subscapularis Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Subscapularis tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Subscapularis Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Subscapularis resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.

key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Teres Minor Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Teres Minor characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Teres Minor Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Teres Minor joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Teres Minor Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Teres Minor-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Teres Minor Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Teres Minor muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Teres Minor Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Teres Minor bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Teres Minor Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Teres Minor due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Long Head of Biceps Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Long Head of Biceps tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Long Head of Biceps Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Long Head of Biceps resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Long Head of Biceps Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Long Head of Biceps with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Long Head of Biceps Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Long Head of Biceps, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Long Head of Biceps Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Long Head of Biceps, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Long Head of Biceps Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Long Head of Biceps during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Subacromial Bursa Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Subacromial Bursa muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Subacromial Bursa Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Subacromial Bursa bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Subacromial Bursa Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Subacromial Bursa due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Acromioclavicular Joint tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Acromioclavicular Joint resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Acromioclavicular Joint with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Acromioclavicular Joint, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Acromioclavicular Joint, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Acromioclavicular Joint during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Acromioclavicular Joint, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Acromioclavicular Joint characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Acromioclavicular Joint Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Acromioclavicular Joint joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.

diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Sternoclavicular Joint resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Sternoclavicular Joint with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Sternoclavicular Joint, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Sternoclavicular Joint, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Sternoclavicular Joint during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Sternoclavicular Joint, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Sternoclavicular Joint characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Sternoclavicular Joint joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Sternoclavicular Joint-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Sternoclavicular Joint muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Sternoclavicular Joint bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Sternoclavicular Joint Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Sternoclavicular Joint due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Deltoid, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Deltoid during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Deltoid, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Deltoid characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Deltoid joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Deltoid-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Deltoid muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Deltoid bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Deltoid Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Deltoid due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Latissimus Dorsi Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Latissimus Dorsi tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Shoulder structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.
