key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Extensor Origin Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Common Extensor Origin muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Extensor Origin Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Common Extensor Origin bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Extensor Origin Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Common Extensor Origin due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Common Flexor Origin tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Common Flexor Origin resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Common Flexor Origin with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Common Flexor Origin, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Common Flexor Origin, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Common Flexor Origin during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Common Flexor Origin, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Common Flexor Origin characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Common Flexor Origin Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Common Flexor Origin joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.

name: Distal Triceps Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Distal Triceps tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Distal Triceps resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Distal Triceps with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Distal Triceps, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Distal Triceps, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Distal Triceps during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Distal Triceps, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Distal Triceps characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Distal Triceps joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Distal Triceps-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Distal Triceps muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Distal Triceps bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Distal Triceps Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Distal Triceps due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Ulnar Collateral Ligament, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Ulnar Collateral Ligament during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Ulnar Collateral Ligament, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Ulnar Collateral Ligament characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Ulnar Collateral Ligament joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Ulnar Collateral Ligament-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Ulnar Collateral Ligament muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Ulnar Collateral Ligament bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Collateral Ligament Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Ulnar Collateral Ligament due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radial Collateral Ligament Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Radial Collateral Ligament tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radial Collateral Ligament Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Radial Collateral Ligament resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radial Collateral Ligament Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Radial Collateral Ligament with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radial Collateral Ligament Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Radial Collateral Ligament, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

description: Degenerative joint disease of the Radiocapitellar Joint characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radiocapitellar Joint Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Radiocapitellar Joint joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radiocapitellar Joint Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Radiocapitellar Joint-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radiocapitellar Joint Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Radiocapitellar Joint muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radiocapitellar Joint Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Radiocapitellar Joint bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Radiocapitellar Joint Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Radiocapitellar Joint due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Annular Ligament tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Annular Ligament resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Annular Ligament with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Annular Ligament, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Annular Ligament, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Annular Ligament during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Elbow structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Annular Ligament Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Annular Ligament, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Median Nerve Stress Fracture
description: Fatigue failure of the Median Nerve bone due to repetitive submaximal loading.
key_findings: Insidious onset of activity-related pain that progresses to rest pain, focal bony tenderness, possible swelling.
diagnostic_tips: MRI is gold standard (X-ray often negative for first 2-3 weeks). Address training load, nutrition, and biomechanics.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Median Nerve Avulsion Injury
description: Tearing away of bone fragment at the attachment site of the Median Nerve due to sudden tensile load.
key_findings: Acute sharp pain at attachment, swelling, weakness on resisted contraction, X-ray shows displaced fragment.
diagnostic_tips: Surgical consult if displacement >2cm. Common sites: ASIS (sartorius), AIIS (rectus femoris), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Tendinopathy
description: Degenerative tendon disorder of the Ulnar Nerve tendon characterised by collagen disorganisation, neovascularisation, and load-related pain.
key_findings: Localised tendon pain on loading, morning stiffness <30 min, palpation tenderness over the tendon, often pain-free at rest in early stages.
diagnostic_tips: Distinguish from peritendinitis (crepitus) and partial-thickness tear (focal weakness). Imaging (US/MRI) shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic areas, neovessels.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Strain
description: Acute muscular injury of the Ulnar Nerve resulting in fibre disruption ranging from microscopic (Grade I) to complete rupture (Grade III).
key_findings: Sudden sharp pain during activity, focal tenderness, pain on stretch and resisted contraction, possible bruising in 24-72h.
diagnostic_tips: Grade by clinical exam: I = pain, no weakness; II = pain + weakness, no full rupture; III = palpable defect, marked weakness. Ultrasound confirms grading.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Ulnar Nerve with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Ulnar Nerve, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Bursitis
description: Inflammation of a bursa adjacent to the Ulnar Nerve, producing localised pain and impaired motion.
key_findings: Localised swelling, warmth, tenderness over bursa, painful arc if subacromial.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out septic bursitis (fever, marked redness) — aspiration if suspected. US confirms fluid collection.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Impingement Syndrome
description: Compression and inflammation of soft tissues around the Ulnar Nerve during specific movements.
key_findings: Painful arc, positive impingement tests (e.g., Neer/Hawkins for shoulder), pain on end-range loading.
diagnostic_tips: Subacromial impingement is a clinical diagnosis. Consider rotator cuff related shoulder pain (RCRSP) terminology (Lewis 2016).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Capsulitis
description: Inflammation and progressive fibrosis of the joint capsule around the Ulnar Nerve, leading to global motion restriction.
key_findings: Capsular pattern of restriction (e.g., ER>ABD>IR at shoulder), night pain, painful at end-range in all directions.
diagnostic_tips: Self-limiting (12-24 months) but can be shortened with intra-articular corticosteroid + physiotherapy in early phase.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Osteoarthritis
description: Degenerative joint disease of the Ulnar Nerve characterised by cartilage loss, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes.
key_findings: Activity-related pain, morning stiffness <30 min, crepitus, gradual loss of motion, possible deformity in advanced disease.
diagnostic_tips: Diagnosis is clinical (NICE 2022); imaging only if atypical features. Exercise & weight loss are first-line per OARSI guidelines.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Synovitis
description: Inflammation of the synovial lining of the Ulnar Nerve joint.
key_findings: Joint warmth, swelling, painful active and passive ROM, morning stiffness >30 min if inflammatory.
diagnostic_tips: Rule out infection (joint aspiration if febrile, marked warmth). Rheumatology referral if multiple joints involved.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Nerve Entrapment
description: Compression of the Ulnar Nerve-related peripheral nerve causing pain, paraesthesia, and possible motor deficit.
key_findings: Dermatomal/peripheral nerve distribution pain, paraesthesia, weakness in innervated muscles, positive provocation test (e.g., Tinel's, Phalen's).
diagnostic_tips: Confirm with nerve conduction studies. Conservative management first; surgical decompression if severe motor loss or failed conservative care >6 months.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.

treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Ulnar Nerve Myofascial Trigger Point Syndrome
description: Hyperirritable spots within the Ulnar Nerve muscle producing referred pain and dysfunction.
key_findings: Palpable taut band, jump sign, predictable referred pain pattern, restricted ROM.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate active (refers spontaneously) vs latent (only on palpation). Address perpetuating factors for lasting relief (Travell & Simons).

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Scapholunate Ligament Tear (Partial-Thickness)
description: Partial-thickness rupture of the Scapholunate Ligament with preserved continuity but functional loss.
key_findings: Acute pain at injury, weakness on resisted testing, painful arc if rotator cuff, MRI/US shows partial discontinuity.
diagnostic_tips: Differentiate from full-thickness tear via active vs passive ROM gap and imaging. Conservative management often successful.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
treatment_plan.chronic: Heavy resistance training, plyometric loading if appropriate, sport/work-specific reconditioning, address psychosocial factors with CBT-informed approaches if persistent.

differential_diagnosis: Consider adjacent Wrist & Hand structures, referred pain, inflammatory arthropathies, and red flag pathologies (tumour, infection, fracture).

name: Scapholunate Ligament Tear (Full-Thickness)
description: Complete rupture of the Scapholunate Ligament, with loss of continuity and significant functional impairment.
causes: Forced eccentric loading on degenerated tissue
key_findings: Sudden pain followed by weakness, palpable defect, marked loss of function, atrophy if chronic.
diagnostic_tips: MRI/US is gold standard. Surgical consult typically warranted, especially in active patients <65 years.

treatment_plan.acute: Relative rest from aggravating loads, education on pain neuroscience, ice or heat per symptom response, gentle pain-free ROM, isometric loading at 70% MVIC for analgesia.
treatment_plan.subacute: Progressive loading (HSR/eccentric), motor control retraining, addressing kinetic chain deficits, manual therapy as adjunct, return-to-activity criteria-based progression.
